The rhizomes contain a starch that has been commercially produced as an easily digestible food ingredient — historically valued for the sick and for infants — and marketed in Australia as 'Queensland arrowroot.'
wildlife_value: The Golden Canna is one of the park's best hummingbird plants. The large, vivid yellow flowers are shaped and colored for hummingbird pollination — Ruby-throated Hummingbirds are primary pollinators, hovering at the blooms in spring and summer. Bees and large butterflies also visit. It is a larval host for the Brazilian (or Canna) skipper, whose caterpillars roll a leaf into a protective tube; dragonfly larvae have also been recorded sheltering in the foliage. The seeds — extraordinarily hard and dense — are eaten by birds and waterfowl when accessible. The broad, paddle-shaped leaves provide cover at the pond edge for frogs, lizards, and small wading birds, and the rhizome mass stabilizes soft pond margins and supports aquatic invertebrates. Because the park's planting sits right at the edge of Cypress Pond, it is doing this ecological work in almost exactly the freshwater-wetland setting it evolved for.

blocks.label: Reproduction
blocks.text: Primarily reproduces through seeds, which require scarification or soaking to germinate due to the exceptionally hard seed coat. Also spreads by rhizomes.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Large, yellow, 3 inches long, in terminal clusters atop stalks reaching 6 feet. Hermaphroditic and self-fertile. Peak bloom spring through summer. Primary pollinators: Ruby-throated Hummingbirds and large bees. Bats also documented as pollinators.

blocks.label: Seeds
blocks.text: Round, very hard, dark brown to black — described as 'like ball bearings.' Among the hardest seeds of any flowering plant.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Large, oblong, blue-green, paddle-shaped — similar to a Canna lily but softer in color than most garden cultivars.

blocks.label: Rhizomes
blocks.text: Fleshy, starch-rich, spreading slowly.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: The tall yellow flower spikes rising above broad blue-green leaves at the water's edge are distinctive in Florida's wetland flora. Very few native wetland plants produce flowers this large and this yellow. Rattle a mature seed pod — the hard seeds knock audibly against each other.

size.spread: spreading colony by rhizomes over time
size.habit: upright, clump-forming wetland perennial

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: wetland species; wet to saturated soils; standing water tolerated
growing_conditions.drought_tolerance: low — requires consistently moist to wet conditions
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: dies back in freezes, resprouts from rhizomes; USDA zones 7–11
growing_conditions.note: native to freshwater wetlands — not salt tolerant

edibility.detail: Edible with preparation — rhizomes yield a digestible starch used historically as food for the sick and infants. Raw rhizomes edible. Seeds edible but essentially impenetrable without cracking. Not a common food plant today.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity. All parts edible with proper preparation.
toxicity.dogs: Not known to be toxic to dogs.

invasive.notes: No. Florida native, not invasive.

species.other_notes: PARK CONTEXT (confirmed): The park's only native cannas — Canna flaccida — introduced by Cypress Pond in 2023. Spread from ~10 to 100+ plants, then knocked back to ~20 by ~1 ft saltwater flooding from a 2024 hurricane (low salt tolerance); survivors slowly recovering. Deliberately kept separate from the non-native ornamental cannas (C. indica) by the office. Cross-reference: Canna Lilies row, PSBP-00041.

quick_hits: Explodes into bloom with thick, powdery clouds of white pollen that look exactly like a winter snowstorm swirling through the tropical garden.
A rare botanical mystery; it pulls nitrogen straight from the air to naturally fertilize the soil beneath it without traditional root nodules.
The smooth, emerald-green upper collar is not a trunk. It is a crownshaft of tightly wrapped leaf bases that falls off when the palm self-cleans.
Standing as Cuba's national tree and Miami-Dade’s official symbol, this iconic silhouette naturally grows only in Florida's extreme southern tip.
species.origin: Native to Mexico, the Caribbean, Florida, and parts of Central America. In Florida naturally found in moist rich hammocks of extreme South Florida. The genus is named for Roy Stone, a US Army general. Also the national tree of Cuba where it is a culturally significant symbol. One of the most majestic palms in the world.
more_information:
The Iconic Tropical Silhouette
The Royal Palm is the definitive living symbol of Florida’s tropical identity and serves as Cuba's proud national tree [1]. During the 19th century, it was planted extensively to frame the grand boulevards of Havana [1]. Its designation as Miami-Dade County’s official tree is well-earned; no other plant defines a tropical horizon from a distance quite like a uniform row of mature Royal Palms [1]. Because wild populations in Florida are naturally restricted to the sub-tropical hammocks of Collier, Miami-Dade, and Monroe counties, this park’s specimen thrives as a striking geographic traveler planted north of its native range.

Botanical Mystery & Summer Snows
Beyond its stately architecture, the Royal Palm possesses fascinating biological traits. When it enters its blooming cycle, the tree releases powdery white pollen in such extreme, massive quantities that the falling particles genuinely resemble a localized winter snowstorm—a striking spectacle documented to stop visitors in their tracks at the historic Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden.

Furthermore, it stands as a major botanical anomaly: it is one of the very few non-legumes verified to fix atmospheric nitrogen. While it lacks the traditional root nodule bacteria found in the pea and bean family, it successfully extracts nitrogen from the air to enrich the surrounding soil, though the exact cellular mechanism remains a subject of ongoing scientific study.

Self-Cleaning Crownshaft
Visitors often mistake the vibrant green column directly beneath the fronds for part of the structural trunk. In reality, this is a crownshaft, formed entirely by the massive, tightly overlapping sheathing bases of the leaves [1]. As the palm grows, it naturally self-cleans; the oldest frond dies, pulls away, and drops the corresponding green sleeve to the ground, cleanly exposing the smooth, concrete-like, gray-white cylinder of the true trunk below [1].
wildlife_value: The Royal Palm produces enormous quantities of small dark-red to black fruits that are critically important to White-crowned Pigeons — a species that depends heavily on tropical fruit and specifically seeks Royal Palm groves. Parrots, grackles, raccoons, and squirrels also take the fruit. When the tree blooms, the pollen release can be so heavy it resembles falling snow — creating a brief but intense resource for bees and pollinating insects. The self-cleaning frond drops provide ground-level material that shelters invertebrates. As one of the few non-legumes that fixes nitrogen into the soil, it also improves the growing conditions of surrounding plants.

blocks.label: Self-cleaning
blocks.text: fronds drop cleanly on their own at a rate of about one per month. The falling fronds are 10 to 15 feet long and can weigh up to 50 pounds — plant away from buildings and pedestrian paths.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Tiny white flowers in showy hanging sprays up to 2 feet long, emerging just below the crownshaft. Pollen release can be so heavy it resembles falling snow. Followed by large clusters of fruit.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Small, dark red to black. Consumed by White-crowned Pigeons, parrots, raccoons, and other wildlife. Handle fruit with rubber gloves — pulp contains calcium oxalate crystals that irritate skin.

blocks.label: Trunk
blocks.text: Smooth, light gray-white, up to 2 feet in diameter. Ringed with evenly-spaced leaf scars.

blocks.label: Crownshaft
blocks.text: The smooth green column at the top of the trunk, formed by overlapping leaf bases — not part of the trunk itself.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: The crownshaft color and smoothness distinguish this from other large palms. Look for the transition line between the gray trunk and the green crownshaft. In fruit, watch the base of the crownshaft for the heavy pendulous fruit clusters. Check for White-crowned Pigeon activity during fruiting season.

size.height: typically 50 to 70 feet; can reach 100 feet
size.habit: solitary, straight pale-gray trunk often bulging at the base, topped by a green crownshaft
size.texture: bold; fronds 10 to 15 feet long, weighing over 50 pounds

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: wide range, well-drained
growing_conditions.salt_tolerance: moderate to salt spray; intolerant of high salt in the root zone
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: hardy to about 28°F; USDA zones 10-11
growing_conditions.note: self-cleaning — about one frond drops per month; fronds are extremely heavy and can damage property, so do not plant close to buildings or walkways

edibility.detail: Used for thatch, construction timber, and in some traditional cooking preparations. Handle fruit with rubber gloves — calcium oxalate crystals in the pulp are highly irritating to skin. Not a human food plant.

toxicity.people: Handle fruit with rubber gloves — pulp contains calcium oxalate crystals that cause significant skin and mucous membrane irritation. Not toxic if fruit is incidentally contacted, but direct handling of pulp without protection is inadvisable.
toxicity.dogs: Not known to be toxic to dogs. Fruit pulp contains oxalate crystals — avoid letting dogs chew fallen fruit.

invasive.notes: No. Florida native. Regarded as potentially weedy in Panama and some other non-native locations — not a concern in Florida.

quick_hits: When a Royal Poinciana blooms, it earns its other name — Flamboyant Tree. The canopy can be entirely covered in vermillion flowers, visible from blocks away.
Native to Madagascar, where it's now considered vulnerable in the wild despite being planted across every tropical city on Earth.
Look closely at the flowers—the top fifth petal is splashed with yellow and white to serve as a literal visual landing strip for pollinators.
In Caribbean communities, the blooming of the Poinciana is a cultural calendar event marking the start of summer.
species.origin: Native to Madagascar but widely planted in tropical and semi-tropical areas worldwide. The origin was unknown to science until the 1930s when a natural colony was discovered on the west coast of Madagascar. Common name honors M. de Poinci, 17th century governor of the French West Indies.
more_information: In full bloom the canopy can be so densely covered with flowers that the leaves become invisible — the entire tree appears to be on fire, explaining the alternate name Flamboyant.

It is the national flower of St. Kitts and Nevis and the national tree of Puerto Rico. For centuries it had been cultivated throughout the tropics but nobody knew where it originally came from — a tree celebrated worldwide whose homeland was a mystery for 400 years.
Patience is required — trees usually need 5 to 10 years to reach blooming age — but the wait is legendary.
Cultivated worldwide for centuries, its true wild homeland was completely unknown to science until it was finally traced back to Madagascar in the 1820s.
Though fiercely protected in parks globally, this tree is endangered in its native Madagascar. Human cultivation is the only reason it survived extinction.
After the fiery blooms fade, the tree drops massive, two-foot-long woody seed pods that rattle when shaken and are used as traditional Caribbean musical instruments.
wildlife_value: The royal poinciana's blazing canopy is more than a show — its claw-shaped flowers are shaped to dust visiting birds and insects with pollen, drawing bees, butterflies, and nectar-seeking birds all summer, while the wide ferny crown throws cooling shade and gives birds perches and shelter. Its real conservation story is survival: royal poincianas are rare and threatened in their native Madagascar, yet so widely planted elsewhere that cultivated trees like this one have become a genuine safety net for the species. In a sense, every healthy specimen helps keep a Madagascan treasure from vanishing. It's a reminder that an ornamental tree can also be an act of conservation.

blocks.label: Briefly deciduous
blocks.text: drops leaves in late winter or dry season before dramatic bloom, then leafs out fully after peak flowering.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Brilliant scarlet-orange, 3 to 4 inches wide, in large terminal clusters. Five petals — four spreading and one upright 'standard' petal streaked with white and yellow. Peak bloom May through July. Bloom is often triggered by a distinct dry or cool season.

blocks.label: Seed pods
blocks.text: Flattened, dark brown, 1 to 2 feet long, persisting through winter and rattling audibly in the wind when dry. Seeds inside are hard and bean-like.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Feathery, bipinnate — up to 20 pairs of primary leaflets, each carrying 25 to 35 pairs of tiny secondary leaflets. The effect is a lacy, fern-like canopy that creates dappled shade.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: In bloom (May through July), the canopy can be entirely hidden under flowers — visible and recognizable from blocks away. After bloom, watch for the long rattling seed pods. Run a finger along the underside of a compound leaf to feel the fine-textured leaflets that give the canopy its feathery quality.

size.spread: 40 to 60 feet — typically much wider than tall at maturity
size.habit: broad, flat-topped, spreading umbrella crown
size.growth_rate: fast — can reach 30 feet in 10 years
size.texture: fine and feathery in leaf; dramatic bold in flower

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: well-drained; tolerates poor, dry, and rocky soils
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: frost-sensitive; damaged below 28°F; USDA zones 10B–11
growing_conditions.note: trees usually require 5 to 10 years from planting to first bloom; large seed pods are persistent litter — plan placement accordingly

edibility.detail: Not edible. No part of the plant is used as food.

toxicity.people: No confirmed toxicity. Large seed pods are persistent litter — not a food hazard but notable near paths.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA.

invasive.notes: Not officially listed as invasive in Florida. Invasive in parts of Australia. Monitor volunteer seedlings.

species.category: Plants to Watch and Invasive Awareness
quick_hits: One of Florida's problem invaders: birds eat the red berries and drop the seeds in wild hammocks, where a seedling can take root high in a native tree and slowly **strangle** it.
In summer the canopy puts out dramatic three-foot-wide clusters of deep red flower spikes that radiate like the arms of an **octopus**.
Originally from Queensland and New Guinea, it was a wildly popular ornamental before its **invasive** potential was understood — the park keeps one as a teaching specimen for exactly that reason.
Palma Sola once had two of these about twenty yards apart; the 2024 saltwater floods claimed one, and the **survivor** is steadily fighting its way back to life.
species.origin: Native to tropical rainforests of Australia, New Guinea, and Java. Introduced to Florida as a landscape and houseplant — once extremely popular, now recognized as a serious invasive problem.
more_information: The Umbrella Tree's story is a cautionary tale in Florida horticulture. For years it was a staple landscape plant — easy, fast, dramatic, widely sold. In fact, it remains one of the most common houseplants in North America — perfectly well-behaved in a pot up north, yet an aggressive invader in frost-free South Florida. Then ecologists began finding it in hammocks where no one had planted it. The mechanism was birds: the red berries are eagerly eaten and the seeds deposited intact in natural areas, sometimes miles away. Young Scheffleras start as epiphytes — germinating in the crotch of a native tree, sending roots down the bark — before eventually overtopping and strangling the host. By the time the problem was recognized, it was already established across South Florida's most sensitive hammock habitats. It is now a FISC Category I invasive, the highest designation, and IFAS classifies it as invasive in both Central and South Florida.

Large established individuals (over 10 inches trunk diameter) are particularly difficult to eradicate — the recommended treatment is cut-stump application of Triclopyr, which can take up to 9 months to kill a large tree. The park's specimen is maintained as an educational display. It is worth keeping an eye on any volunteer seedlings in surrounding areas.

Note: the current accepted scientific name is *Heptapleurum actinophyllum* — the genus was reclassified from Schefflera based on molecular (DNA) analysis showing it is distinct from the true Scheffleras. Most literature and signage still uses Schefflera actinophylla.
wildlife_value: The Umbrella Tree's red berries are actively consumed by birds — which is precisely what makes it invasive. Mockingbirds, robins, and many other fruit-eating species disperse its seeds widely. The large compound leaves create heavy shade that reduces ground-level plant diversity beneath it. While it provides fruiting resources and canopy cover, its ecological value in Florida is outweighed by the habitat displacement it causes in natural areas. As an educational invasive specimen, it demonstrates exactly how bird-dispersed fruiting plants can escape cultivation — a lesson worth teaching to every visitor who walks past it.

blocks.label: Produces flowers in summer when grown in full sun
blocks.text: an unusual radial arrangement of multiple flower spikes extending from a central point, resembling the legs of an octopus (one of its common names). Can start as an epiphyte, germinating in tree crotches before rooting to the ground.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small and dark red, borne on radial clusters of stems up to three feet across.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Small, round, dark red to purple, produced prolifically. Highly attractive to birds — the primary dispersal vector.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Large, palmately compound with 7 to 16 glossy oval leaflets arranged in a circular pattern. Each leaflet up to 12 inches long.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: The circular arrangement of leaflets on long petioles creates the 'umbrella' silhouette the name describes — look up into the crown from below for the full effect. The octopus-like flower stalks in summer are distinctive. Watch for volunteer seedlings — small umbrella-leaved plants appearing in unexpected locations.

size.habit: single or multi-stemmed; large palmately compound leaves with 7 to 16 leaflets

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: wide range of soils
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: USDA zones 9B-11
growing_conditions.growth_habit: grows terrestrially or as an epiphyte
growing_conditions.note: bird-dispersed fruits; invasive in hammocks, particularly wet rocky sites — see Invasive Notes

edibility.detail: Not edible.

toxicity.people: Contains calcium oxalate crystals and other compounds causing significant mouth and throat irritation if ingested. Keep away from children and pets. Sap can cause skin irritation in sensitive individuals.
toxicity.dogs: Toxic to dogs. Schefflera contains insoluble calcium oxalates causing oral pain, drooling, vomiting, and difficulty swallowing (ASPCA).

invasive.notes: FISC Category I invasive — the highest designation. IFAS Assessment: Invasive in Central and South Florida. Maintained here as an educational specimen only and have NOT seen invasive behavior. Monitor surrounding area for volunteer seedlings and remove promptly. Do not propagate or plant elsewhere on the property.

species.other_notes: In its years at Palma Sola — in the park's particular microclimate — this species has shown no sign of the invasive behavior it's known for elsewhere. We keep a close watch for volunteer seedlings all the same, and never propagate or plant it elsewhere on the property.
species.internal_notes: Monitor for volunteer seedlings in surrounding hammock and natural areas.

species.category: Plants to Watch and Invasive Awareness
alternate_names: (also sold as Ruellia brittoniana, R. tweediana, R. caerulea)
quick_hits: Mexican Petunia is one of Florida's most commonly planted purple-flowered ornamentals — and one of its most problematic invasives.
It spreads two ways at once: underground runners that form dense mats, and seed capsules that use tiny spring-loaded biological levers to catapult seeds several feet when wetted by rain.
It is a FISC (formerly FLEPPC) Category I invasive — the highest designation — listed since 2001.
UF/IFAS has bred sterile cultivars like the Mayan series so gardeners can keep the look without the ecological harm.
species.origin: Native to Mexico, South America, and the Caribbean. Found naturally in moist disturbed ground, riverbanks, and wetland edges. Introduced to Florida before 1940 and once one of the most widely sold landscape plants in the state.
more_information: Mexican Petunia is a textbook case of a beautiful plant that became an ecological problem. It is a FISC Category I invasive — the worst category, reserved for plants that alter how natural ecosystems function by displacing native species and changing community structure. The concern is sharpest in Florida wetlands, where it can shift not just plant-animal relationships but the hydrology of an entire watershed.

Its success comes from a rare combination of traits. It produces seed year-round with no need for cold or scarification, severed stem pieces root readily — so mowing or weed-eating only spreads it — and its seed-launching machinery is genuinely remarkable. Like other members of the Acanthaceae family, each capsule holds its seeds on tiny hook-shaped levers called retinacula (or jaculators). When rain wets the ripe capsule it splits open and these levers catapult the seeds outward. Once a seed lands on damp ground, microscopic hairs on its coat swell into a sticky mucilage that holds moisture against the seed and speeds germination. Removal requires taking out the entire plant, roots and all.

There is a constructive side to the story. Since 2007, a UF/IFAS breeding program led by Rosanna Freyre has developed sterile cultivars — the Mayan series ('Mayan Purple,' 'Mayan White,' 'Mayan Pink,' 'Mayan Compact Purple') plus the older 'Purple Showers' — that give gardeners the same showy flower without the seed-driven invasion. At Palma Sola the plant is presented in the Plants to Watch and Invasive Awareness area precisely so visitors can recognize it and understand why the sterile alternatives matter.
Native alternatives for home gardens include the native Wild Petunia (Ruellia caroliniensis), Blue Curls, Butterfly Weed, and Swamp Milkweed.
wildlife_value: Mexican Petunia's purple trumpet flowers do attract butterflies and some native bees, and it is a nectar source and a minor larval host for certain butterflies. But its wildlife value is outweighed by the harm it does: by forming dense monocultures it displaces the diverse native plant communities that support far richer wildlife, and in wetlands it can crowd out the native species that specialist insects and birds depend on. The honest assessment is that its ecological costs exceed its pollinator benefits — which is why native alternatives are recommended.

blocks.label: Reproduces aggressively by two mechanisms
blocks.text: explosive seed dispersal and spreading rhizomes/runners. Severed stem sections also root readily, so cutting it back spreads rather than controls it.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Purple (occasionally pink or white) trumpet-shaped flowers about 1.5 to 2 inches wide, with five flaring lobes. Each flower lasts a single day, but the plant blooms continuously spring through fall.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Slender seed capsules that hold their seeds on tiny hook-shaped levers (retinacula, or jaculators) — a hallmark of the Acanthaceae family. When a ripe capsule is wetted by rain it splits and the levers catapult the seeds several feet from the parent plant. On contact with damp ground the seed coat releases a sticky mucilage that anchors it and speeds germination. Seeds need no cold or scarification to sprout.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Dark green, lance-shaped, 6 to 12 inches long and ½ to ¾ inch wide, oppositely arranged, with prominent veins on the underside. Margins smooth to slightly wavy. Stems green or purple-tinged.

blocks.label: Roots
blocks.text: Spreading rhizomes and runners form dense, almost impenetrable mats; resprouts vigorously from crowns and root fragments.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Tall upright purple-stemmed clumps topped with five-lobed purple trumpets that carpet the ground below with fallen blooms. Watch for it forming solid stands that exclude everything else — that mat-forming habit is the visual signature of the invasive wild type. Sterile garden cultivars look similar but do not set the seed capsules.

size.height: 2 to 4 feet (dwarf 'Katie' cultivars stay under 1 foot)
size.spread: indefinite — forms expanding colonies by rhizome
size.habit: upright herbaceous perennial, clump- and mat-forming

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: virtually any — thrives from standing water and wetland margins to nearly xeric upland sites
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: root-hardy; USDA zones 8 to 11
growing_conditions.note: extreme adaptability across light, moisture, and soil is exactly what makes it such an effective invader

edibility.detail: Not a food plant for humans. Some keepers report tortoises and poultry browse the foliage, but it has no culinary use.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to humans.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. No specific toxicity data — not known to be harmful.

invasive.notes: FISC (formerly FLEPPC) Category I invasive since 2001 — the highest designation. UF/IFAS does not recommend planting the wild type or fertile cultivars anywhere in Florida. Sterile cultivars (Mayan series, 'Purple Showers') are the recommended substitutes. Wild plants must be removed entirely, roots included, as they resprout from fragments.

species.internal_notes:
STAFF NOTE: Discuss management strategy with Bev. Confirm whether park specimens are the wild type or a sterile cultivar — this determines seed-spread risk and signage messaging.

TO INVESTIGATE: Confirm whether park specimens are the wild type or one of the UF/IFAS sterile cultivars — 'Purple Showers' (sterile, older), or the Mayan series 'Mayan Purple,' 'Mayan White,' 'Mayan Pink,' 'Mayan Compact Purple' (sterile, released 2012-2013). All other named cultivars ('Chi Chi,' 'Snow White,' the dwarf 'Katie' types) are fertile and still invasive. Randy suspects ours are NOT the sterile types. If wild/fertile, the explosive-seed messaging applies; if sterile, the sign should note seeds are not produced.

quick_hits: Stand by the office and you're being welcomed by a milkweed — this is the big silver-leaved shrub that greets visitors coming up from the parking lot, and another large one rings the butterfly garden.
It's a Monarch and Queen butterfly host plant: caterpillars eat the leaves and absorb the plant's toxins, which makes the adult butterflies poisonous to birds.
Its milky sap is toxic — the same class of heart-affecting compounds found in Oleander — so it's handled with care even as the butterflies feed on it freely.
It roots so willingly from cuttings that the park propagates new plants from these specimens regularly.
species.origin: Native to South Asia and Southeast Asia — India, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Pakistan, and neighboring regions — and widely naturalized across the tropics. Grows naturally in dry, open, disturbed ground, roadsides, and sandy coastal soils.
more_information: The Giant Milkweed is one of the most generous butterfly plants in the collection — and one of the most photogenic, with thick, pale silver-green leaves and clusters of waxy, crown-shaped lavender-and-white flowers. At Palma Sola it does double duty as a greeter: the specimen by the office welcomes visitors walking up from the parking lot, and a second large plant rings the butterfly garden. Both are propagated regularly from cuttings, which this plant produces with unusual ease — a snipped stem stuck in moist soil roots readily.

There's a fitting bit of symbolism in a milkweed serving as a welcomer. Its other common name is Crown Flower, for the small crown shape at the center of each bloom, and in Hawaii — where it arrived in the 1880s and naturalized — the flowers are strung into leis of welcome and celebration. They were the favorite flower of Queen Liliʻuokalani, Hawaii's last reigning monarch, who is said to have loved them for their royal crown shape.

The genus name Calotropis comes from the Greek kalos ('beautiful') and tropis ('boat keel'), describing the shape of the flower parts; gigantea simply means large. The plant has a long history in traditional medicine across its native range, where the latex was even used to help work thorns out of skin — though every part is toxic and none of this should be tried at home.

A note on its milkweeds: this is a non-native milkweed. It feeds Monarch and Queen caterpillars enthusiastically, but for supporting Florida's native Monarch populations specifically, native milkweeds like Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) and Swamp Milkweed are also worth planting alongside it.
wildlife_value: The Giant Milkweed is a powerhouse butterfly host. Its leaves feed the caterpillars of Monarch, Queen, and Soldier butterflies — all milkweed specialists — and a single established plant can feed an army of Monarch larvae, regrowing quickly after they strip it. The caterpillars sequester the plant's toxic cardenolides into their bodies, carrying that chemical defense into adulthood so birds learn to avoid them. The large waxy flowers are nectar sources worked by butterflies and large bees, including carpenter bees (Xylocopa). Because it blooms nearly year-round in Florida, it offers an unusually steady supply of both larval food and nectar — which is exactly why it earns its place greeting visitors and anchoring the butterfly garden.

blocks.label: Propagates very easily from stem cuttings
blocks.text: a snipped stem in moist soil roots readily, which is how the park grows new plants from its specimens. Also grows from seed carried on silky parachutes.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Clusters of waxy, five-petaled flowers in lavender-purple or white, each about ¾ inch across, with a distinctive raised crown-shaped center. The pollen is bundled into waxy sacs (pollinia) that clip onto the legs of visiting bees — an elaborate insect-pollination mechanism shared with other milkweeds. Blooms nearly year-round, heaviest in summer.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Large, inflated, boat-shaped seed pods (follicles) 3 to 4 inches long, splitting to release numerous seeds, each carried on a tuft of silky white hairs.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Large, thick, opposite, pale gray-green and slightly waxy with a fine fuzz, up to 8 inches long. The silvery foliage is the plant's most recognizable feature from a distance.

blocks.label: Sap
blocks.text: Thick, milky white latex in all parts — toxic and a skin and eye irritant.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A big, somewhat open shrub with bold silver-gray leaves — unmistakable from across the lot. Look for the crown-shaped flowers and, on the leaves, the real prize: fat Monarch and Queen caterpillars in bold warning stripes, often with chewed leaf edges nearby. Snap nothing — the white sap stains and irritates. Watch for the inflated pods releasing silk-borne seeds in fall.

size.habit: large, irregular, somewhat open shrub; benefits from periodic pruning to stay shapely
size.texture: coarse — bold pale gray-green leaves up to 8 inches long

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: well-drained; thrives in dry, sandy, even poor soils
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: frost-sensitive; damaged below freezing but rebounds fast from wind or cold damage; USDA zones 9B-11
growing_conditions.note: propagates very easily from cuttings; few pests or diseases

edibility.detail: Not edible — toxic in all parts. The milky sap contains heart-affecting cardenolide glycosides. Despite traditional medicinal uses in its native range, no part should be eaten or applied at home.

toxicity.people: Toxic. All parts contain cardenolide glycosides similar to those in Oleander. The milky latex causes severe skin and eye irritation, and ingestion can cause serious cardiac effects. Keep away from children. Wear gloves when pruning or taking cuttings, and keep sap away from eyes.
toxicity.dogs: Toxic to dogs. The cardenolide glycosides in the sap and foliage can cause vomiting, drooling, and serious heart-rhythm effects if ingested (consistent with ASPCA warnings for cardiac-glycoside milkweeds). Keep dogs from chewing leaves or stems.

invasive.notes: Not currently on Florida's FISC invasive list, but naturalized in some warm coastal areas — worth monitoring. Spreads modestly by silk-borne seed. Not aggressive in the way the park's listed invasives are.

species.other_notes: PARK CONTEXT: Specimen by the office greets visitors from the parking lot; a second large plant rings the butterfly garden. Park propagates new plants from these specimens by cuttings regularly. Wear gloves when taking cuttings — sap is a cardiac-glycoside irritant.

quick_hits: In our cactus garden you can see this plant's entire life story at once: young rosettes still building energy, a living giant with its towering bloom stalk, and a fully browned skeleton that has finished the job and died.
The name is a tall tale — it doesn't live a century, just 10 to 30 years — but it spends that whole life saving up for a single, spectacular bloom.
When it finally flowers, the stalk emerges like a gargantuan asparagus spear and rockets up as fast as a foot a day, reaching 20 feet or more — then the whole plant dies, having bloomed exactly once.
It doesn't truly vanish: pups sprout around the base to carry the colony on, so the cycle you see here keeps repeating.
species.origin: Native to Mexico and the southwestern United States, where it grows in arid, rocky, well-drained ground. One of the most widely cultivated succulents in the world and naturalized across every warm continent.
more_information: The Century Plant's life is one of nature's great dramatic gestures: decades of patient, slow growth in service of a single season of spectacular reproduction, followed by death. This is called a monocarpic life cycle — flowering once, then dying — and the common name comes straight from it. People watching a plant grow for 20 or 30 years without blooming assumed it must take a century.

Two surprises hide in plain sight. First, despite looking like a desert cousin of aloe, the agave is in the asparagus family — and it shows: when the bloom finally fires, the stalk pushes out of the rosette looking exactly like a gargantuan spear of asparagus before it shoots skyward. Second, this is a night-breather. To survive blazing sun without losing water, it keeps the pores on its leaves shut all day and opens them only at night to take in the air it needs — which is how a plant this lush thrives where little else can.

At Palma Sola the cactus garden does something a single plant never could: it shows all three phases of that life cycle side by side. There are younger rosettes still storing energy, at least one living giant in or near its towering flush of bloom, and a fully dead specimen — browned top to bottom with its spent stalk still standing. Walk between them and you are walking through the plant's entire biography in a few steps.

When the bloom finally comes, the flowering stalk can grow up to a foot per day — fast enough to notice day to day — and tops out at 20 to 25 feet, one of the tallest flower stalks of any plant. In its native range those night-opening, nectar-rich flowers are pollinated chiefly by nectar-feeding bats (the plant is 'chiropterophilous,' or bat-loving), with bees, hawk moths, and birds joining in.

And the plant behind a famous drinks aisle: Agave americana is one of several agaves used to make mezcal and pulque, but true tequila comes specifically from a different species, the Blue Agave (Agave tequilana). The death you see in the dead specimen here isn't quite the end — pups (offsets) sprout around the base of a flowering plant, genetically identical clones that carry the colony forward, sometimes for centuries.
wildlife_value: In its native Mexico and the southwestern US, the Century Plant is a keystone resource for nectar-feeding bats — including the migratory lesser long-nosed bat, which times its journeys to agave bloom and can pollinate dozens of plants in a single night. The towering stalk's thousands of nectar-rich, night-opening flowers also feed bees, hawk moths, hummingbirds, and other birds. In Florida, well outside its bat range, the bloom is still a major nectar event for bees and butterflies. The dense, spiny rosette offers protected shelter for lizards and small creatures, and even the standing dead stalk becomes a perch and a nesting and foraging site for insects and birds.

blocks.label: Monocarpic
blocks.text: flowers a single time at the end of its life, then dies. Reproduces by seed and, more reliably, by pups (offsets) that sprout from the base and continue the colony after the parent dies.

blocks.label: Bloom
blocks.text: After 10 to 30 years, the plant sends up an enormous flowering stalk that emerges looking like a giant asparagus spear, then grows as much as a foot per day, reaching 20 to 25 feet, branching near the top and bearing thousands of yellow flowers.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Six-petaled, yellow, rich in nectar, opening at night; bat-pollinated in the native range, with bees, moths, and birds also visiting.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Thick, succulent, gray-green to blue-green, 3 to 6 feet long, arranged in a massive rosette. Margins lined with hooked teeth and tipped with a single sharp, rigid terminal spine that can pierce deeply.

blocks.label: Pups
blocks.text: Clonal offsets around the base — the plant's continuation after the bloom.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Here, compare the three life stages directly. A young plant is a tight rosette of spine-edged blue-green blades with no stalk. A plant just starting to bloom pushes up what looks like a colossal asparagus spear; a fully flowering one carries the giant branched stalk overhead — look (carefully) for bees working it. The dead plant is browned top to bottom with the bare stalk still standing — and look at its base for the next generation of pups already coming up. Respect the leaf tips: the terminal spine is genuinely dangerous.

size.flowering_stalk: 20 to 25 feet — among the tallest flower stalks of any plant
size.habit: massive solitary rosette that produces basal pups

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: well-drained, any texture; thrives in poor, sandy, rocky soils
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: moderate — damaged by hard freezes; USDA zones 8-11
growing_conditions.note: very low maintenance; site away from paths because of the dangerous leaf spines

edibility.detail: Edible with preparation — historically one of Mexico's most important food and drink plants. The roasted heart (piña), young flower stalk, and sap (aguamiel) are consumed, and fermented sap becomes pulque; distilled, it becomes mezcal. (True tequila comes from a different species, Blue Agave.) Not a casual nibble — raw tissue is irritating and the plant requires roasting or fermentation.

toxicity.people: Caution. The rigid terminal spine causes serious puncture injuries and the leaf margins are toothed. The raw sap contains calcium oxalate crystals and saponins that irritate skin, and can cause phototoxic dermatitis — skin contact followed by sunlight may bring on rash and blistering. Wear long sleeves and gloves when working near it.
toxicity.dogs: Caution for dogs. The sap's saponins and calcium oxalates can cause drooling, vomiting, and mouth irritation if chewed, and the spine-tipped leaves are a physical hazard. Not among the deadly plants, but discourage chewing and contact.

invasive.notes: Not on Florida's FISC invasive lists. Spreads slowly by pups rather than aggressively.

species.other_notes: PARK CONTEXT: The cactus garden intentionally displays all three life-cycle phases at once — young pre-bloom rosettes, a living plant with the large flowering stalk, and a fully dead browned specimen with the spent stalk standing. Strong interpretive teaching opportunity for monocarpic life cycles. Note the pups at the base of the dead plant as the cycle's continuation.

alternate_names: (hybrids often sold as Canna x generalis)
quick_hits: Despite the name, Canna Lilies are not lilies at all — they're the showy tropical relatives of bananas and gingers.
The hard, round seeds are so dense they were once loaded into shotguns as improvised pellets — which is how the plant earned its old name, 'Indian Shot.'
For years these non-native cannas grew hidden among the heliconia by the office — close enough in looks that nobody noticed they weren't heliconia at all, until a trained eye spotted the difference.
They spread so readily by rhizome that volunteers now dig the young cannas out of the heliconia and move them to open ground in the canna area next door.
species.origin: Native to the tropical and subtropical Americas, from Mexico through Central America into South America. Canna × generalis is the wild species behind most ornamental garden cannas. It was one of the first New World ornamentals traded globally, reaching Europe and Asia centuries ago — and the species name indica ('of India') is a historical mix-up, a relic of the old confusion between the East and West Indies.
more_information: The Canna Lily is a plant of cheerful contradictions. It isn't a lily — it belongs to its own family, Cannaceae, in the same order as bananas, gingers, and the bird of paradise — and its scientific name points to the wrong side of the world. Early European botanists, tangled in the centuries-old confusion between the East and West Indies, named the American plant indica, 'of India.' The name stuck even after the error was understood.

At Palma Sola, these non-native cannas come with a hide-and-seek story. For years they grew quietly mixed in among the heliconia by the office — broad paddle leaves close enough to heliconia's that, to the untrained eye, nobody realized there were cannas in there at all. Once you learn the difference it's obvious, and watching a visitor suddenly 'see' the canna they'd been walking past is one of the small pleasures of the bed.

The plants also weathered a hurricane. A canna planting by Cypress Pond was hit hard when storm flooding put that corner of western Bradenton under a foot of saltwater, and cannas — with their low salt tolerance — struggled there afterward. Today the park keeps the two kinds sorted: the non-native cannas here by the office, and the Florida-native Golden Canna (Canna flaccida) re-established over by Cypress Pond.

That sorting is an ongoing teaching moment. Because canna spreads aggressively by rhizome and keeps popping up among the heliconia, our high school volunteers learn to tell the two plants apart and to lift the wandering canna rhizomes carefully — intact, by the rhizome — and transplant them to open ground in the canna area next door, which is filling back in week by week.

Beyond the garden, this is a plant of real human history. Its rhizomes are edible and starchy — grown as a food crop called Queensland arrowroot in parts of Australia and Southeast Asia — and the famously hard seeds have served as beads, as a coffee substitute, and as genuine shotgun pellets. Those seeds are also remarkably long-lived; Canna seeds recovered from old archaeological contexts have germinated after centuries of dormancy.
wildlife_value: Canna Lilies are hummingbird and butterfly plants. The bright red, orange, or yellow flowers are rich in nectar and shaped for hummingbirds, which are primary visitors, while bees and butterflies work them as well. Canna leaves serve as a larval host and shelter for the Brazilian (or Canna) skipper, whose caterpillars roll a leaf into a protective tube around themselves. The dense clumping foliage gives cover to small wildlife at the water's edge, and at Palma Sola the canna-and-heliconia bed by the office reads as one continuous block of tropical nectar and shelter through much of the year.

blocks.label: Reproduction
blocks.text: Spreads readily by underground rhizomes, forming expanding clumps; also reproduces by seed. Easiest to propagate by dividing the rhizomes — which is exactly how the park relocates volunteers that pop up among the heliconia.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Showy red, orange, or yellow blooms in terminal clusters, spring through fall. The colorful 'petals' are actually modified stamens (staminodes). Pollinated chiefly by hummingbirds, with bees and butterflies visiting.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Spiky, warty capsules that split to release hard, round, BB-like black seeds — the 'Indian shot.'

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Large, broad, paddle-shaped, alternate, in green, bronze, or purple depending on cultivar — superficially similar to heliconia and banana foliage, which is exactly why they go unnoticed mixed into a heliconia bed.

blocks.label: Telling canna from heliconia
blocks.text: Canna leaves spiral around an upright stem and the plant arises from a knobby underground rhizome; heliconia leaves tend to fan in a flatter plane from a more clumping base. Up close the difference is clear — the skill our student volunteers are taught.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Bold upright clumps of broad tropical leaves topped with vivid red-orange-yellow flower spikes. By the office, play spot-the-canna: see if you can pick out the cannas tucked in among the heliconia before reading the leaf tips. Look for hummingbirds working the blooms and for rolled-up leaf tubes that signal Canna skipper caterpillars inside, plus the newly transplanted clumps filling in the canna area next door.

size.spread: 2 to 3 feet per clump, expanding by rhizome
size.habit: upright, clump-forming from spreading rhizomes
size.texture: coarse, bold tropical foliage — green, bronze, or purple by cultivar

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: moist, fertile, well-drained; tolerates wet ground and pond margins
growing_conditions.salt_tolerance: low — the Cypress Pond planting was hit hard by hurricane saltwater flooding
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: foliage frost-sensitive; rhizomes survive moderate freezes; USDA zones 7-11
growing_conditions.note: heavy feeder; spreads aggressively by rhizome — easy to divide and relocate

edibility.detail: Edible — the starchy rhizomes are eaten and grown as a food crop ('Queensland arrowroot') in parts of Australia and Southeast Asia. Seeds have been used as a coffee substitute. Not a casual trail nibble, but a genuine food plant with long history.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to humans.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. No specific toxicity data — not known to be harmful.

invasive.notes: Not on Florida's FISC invasive lists. Spreads aggressively by rhizome in garden settings and can escape locally, so contain or divide as needed — but not a listed invasive.

species.other_notes: PARK CONTEXT (confirmed): Non-native ornamental cannas. The only NATIVE cannas at the park are Canna flaccida introduced in 2023 by Cypress Pond (see Golden Canna, PSBP-00034) — everything else is non-native C. indica. These non-native cannas grew for years unnoticed among the heliconia by the office (looked close enough that nobody realized they were cannas). Now deliberately separated: non-native by the office, native by Cypress Pond. A Cypress Pond canna planting was hit by ~1 ft saltwater flooding from a 2024 hurricane; cannas struggled there afterward. High school volunteers are taught to distinguish canna from heliconia and to lift/transplant wandering canna rhizomes intact to the adjacent canna area (flushing back week by week).

quick_hits: The flower spike coils like a **scorpion's tail** and slowly uncurls as each tiny white bloom opens in turn, from base to tip.
It's a Florida native and a year-round **butterfly magnet** — its nectar feeds the Miami blue, Schaus' swallowtail, gulf fritillary, queen, and a long list of others.
It's one of several UF/IFAS-recommended natives Palma Sola is planting along its pond shores for **erosion control** — look for it around most of the park's pond banks.
The genus name Heliotropium means '**sun-turning**,' from the old belief that the flowers follow the sun across the sky.
species.origin: Native to Florida's coastal counties — from Pinellas and Volusia south through the Keys — and on through Texas, Mexico, the West Indies, and Central and South America. Grows naturally in coastal hammocks, strands, and open disturbed ground near the coast.
more_information: The Scorpion Tail's coiled flowering spike is a small marvel of plant engineering. Botanists call it a scorpioid cyme (or cincinnus): the flower axis curls over on itself, and the blossoms open in sequence from the base of the coil toward the tip, so the spike appears to slowly unfurl over days. The common name captures it exactly, and the genus name reaches even further back — Heliotropium comes from the Greek helios ('sun') and trepein ('to turn'), from the old belief that these plants turn their flowers to follow the sun.

At Palma Sola, this modest native has become a workhorse of restoration. The park is deploying it aggressively along its pond shorelines under two complementary grants — from the Tampa Bay Estuary Program and the Sarasota Bay Estuary Program — to restore and stabilize pond edges. UF/IFAS consultants selected it as one of several species that perform well for shoreline stabilization in brackish water, where many ornamentals fail. A plant most people walk past as a roadside weed is, in this setting, doing real ecological work holding the park's shorelines together — and feeding an exceptional range of butterflies while it does (see Wildlife Value).

A note of caution balances the charm: like many in its family, Scorpion Tail contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids, compounds that can harm the liver if the plant is eaten in quantity. It has a history of folk-medicinal use in the Caribbean, but it is not a plant to eat or self-medicate with.
wildlife_value: Scorpion Tail is a premier Florida butterfly plant. Blooming nearly year-round, its white flowers are a steady nectar source for an unusually long roster of butterflies — including two of South Florida's rarest, the federally endangered Miami blue and Schaus' swallowtail, alongside gulf fritillaries, queens, great southern whites, gray hairstreaks, cassius blues, and ruddy daggerwings. Native bees work the flowers, and birds eat the small seeds. Its fast-spreading, soil-binding roots stabilize pond and shoreline edges, while the low branching cover shelters insects, lizards, and small wildlife at the water's margin — habitat value layered on top of its pollinator value.

blocks.label: Reproduction
blocks.text: Fast-growing and a prolific self-seeder; short-lived (often about 3 years) but readily replaces itself. Propagated by seed or cuttings — useful for the volume planting the park's restoration work requires.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Tiny white flowers with pale yellow throats, borne in two rows along one side of a curved, coiling terminal spike (the 'scorpion tail'), which uncurls as blooms open from base to tip. Blooms year-round. Draws butterflies and native bees.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Small dry fruit splitting into seed-bearing nutlets; seeds favored by birds.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Lanceolate to elliptic, alternate, stalked, with deeply veined surfaces and softly hairy undersides.

blocks.label: Stems
blocks.text: Branched, herbaceous to slightly woody, sprawling to upright.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Look for the distinctive coiled flower spike — the namesake scorpion's tail — slowly unfurling as the little white flowers open along it. Watch the blooms for butterfly traffic; this is one of the park's busiest nectar stops. Around most of the park's pond banks, notice it planted in quantity alongside other native shoreline species, knitting the water's edge together.

size.spread: 1 to 3 feet (sometimes broader where it sprawls)
size.habit: branching herbaceous perennial to sub-shrub, upright to sprawling; short-lived but self-seeding
size.texture: fine to medium, with small hairy leaves

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: well-drained sandy, rocky, or calcareous soils; moist to moderately dry
growing_conditions.salt_tolerance: moderate — tolerates brackish shoreline conditions, which is why it suits the park's pond-edge restoration
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: frost-sensitive; USDA zones 9-11
growing_conditions.note: prolific self-seeder; deadheading limits volunteer seedlings; easy from seed or cuttings

edibility.detail: Not edible. Contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids, which can cause liver damage if consumed. Despite folk-medicinal use in parts of the Caribbean, it should not be eaten or used medicinally without expert guidance.

toxicity.people: Caution. Contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids that can harm the liver if consumed in quantity. Not dangerous to brush against or handle, but not a plant to eat or self-medicate with.
toxicity.dogs: Caution for dogs. The plant's pyrrolizidine alkaloids are liver-toxic if eaten in quantity (these compounds are documented as toxic to horses and livestock). Casual contact is not a concern; discourage chewing.

invasive.notes: Florida native; not invasive. Reseeds readily in cultivation but does not invade natural areas.

quick_hits: Despite the name, this isn't a true iris — it's an African cousin in the iris family, built tough enough to shrug off drought, flooding, shade, and salt spray with equal indifference.
Each yellow, black-spotted flower lasts just a single day, but fresh buds open in waves roughly every two weeks — the trait behind its other name, Fortnight Lily.

At Palma Sola it runs in a long row — some ten yards of it — along the Front Pond bank beside a bench, flanked by pond-edge restoration plantings, giving anyone who sits down a front-row seat to the shoreline coming back to life.
It blooms straight off the old flower stalks year after year, so those spent stalks should never be cut off.
species.origin: Native to South Africa, where it grows in open ground and is so tough it's planted along roadsides and in public landscapes throughout its home range. Now one of the most widely used low-maintenance ornamentals in warm climates worldwide.
more_information: The African Iris belongs to the genus Dietes — a small group of South African evergreens in the iris family that are famous, among gardeners, for being nearly impossible to kill. The genus name comes from the Greek for 'having two relatives,' a nod to its kinship with the true irises (Iris) and the African moraeas. Each bloom opens for just one day, but the plant flowers in rhythmic flushes roughly two weeks apart through the warm months, which is how it earned the name Fortnight Lily.

At Palma Sola the African Iris has one of the nicer spots in the park: a long row — roughly ten yards of it — running between a bench and the edge of the Front Pond, so anyone sitting down looks out over the irises to the water. To either side, and across the way along the far bank, are the pond-edge restoration plantings — making the bench effectively a viewing seat for the shoreline the park has been rebuilding. Much of that surrounding planting was likely put in by the park's high school volunteers, who earn college-scholarship service hours doing exactly this kind of restoration work.

One care note that matters: this plant reblooms from the same flower stalks year after year, so the old stalks must not be trimmed off after the flowers fade — cutting them removes next season's blooms. The spent stalks look untidy but should be left alone.
wildlife_value: The African Iris's flowers offer nectar and pollen to bees and other pollinating insects during each two-week flush of bloom. Its dense, clumping fans of stiff evergreen leaves provide year-round ground-level cover for lizards, frogs, and insects — particularly valuable here at the pond edge, where it sits among native restoration plantings and adds to the layered shelter along the bank. While it is a non-native ornamental rather than a wildlife powerhouse, in this setting it contributes structure and bloom to a shoreline being managed for habitat.

blocks.label: Reproduction
blocks.text: Spreads by slowly expanding rhizomes, forming dense clumps over time; also grows from seed. Propagated by dividing the rhizomes after flowering or by sowing seed.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Iris-like blooms on tall, branched stalks — pale yellow with three dark, near-black spots ringed in orange. They open in flushes about every two weeks through the warm season, each flower lasting a single day. Pollinated by bees.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: A capsule that can become heavy enough to bend the flower stalk toward the ground; it dries, splits, and releases dark brown seeds.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Stiff, narrow, sword-like, pale green, evergreen, growing in flat fans from the base of the clump — attractive year-round even out of bloom.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Look for the fans of stiff sword-shaped leaves and the wiry branched flower stalks rising above them — topped with yellow, black-spotted, iris-like flowers. Note the day-only blooms and, lower down, the heavy seed capsules bending the stalks. Crucially, notice that the old flower stalks are left uncut — that's deliberate, because the plant reblooms from them. From the bench, look past the iris to the pond-edge restoration plantings flanking it on both sides and across the water.

size.spread: 2 to 3 feet per clump, expanding slowly by rhizome (planted here as a continuous row ~10 yards long)
size.habit: clumping, evergreen, fan-forming perennial with stiff sword-like leaves

growing_conditions.light: full sun to partial shade (best bloom in sun; tolerates shade with fewer flowers)
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: wide range, well-drained to moist; tolerant of poor soils
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: hardy to about 20°F; USDA zones 8-11
growing_conditions.note: very low maintenance; do NOT cut spent flower stalks — the plant reblooms from them

edibility.detail: Not edible. Contains compounds that cause gastrointestinal upset if ingested.

toxicity.people: Mildly toxic. The Iridaceae family contains irisin and other irritants, particularly in the rhizomes. Ingestion may cause nausea, vomiting, and digestive upset. Avoid eating any part of the plant.
toxicity.dogs: Mildly toxic to dogs and cats. Iridaceae family compounds cause gastrointestinal irritation. Keep pets from digging the rhizomes, which have the highest concentration.

invasive.notes: Not on Florida's FISC invasive lists. (Note: the related Dietes grandiflora is considered an environmental weed in parts of Australia, but not a concern in Florida.)

species.other_notes: PLACEMENT: Front Pond, a continuous row about 10 yards long between a bench and the water, flanked by pond-edge restoration plantings (likely installed by high school scholarship volunteers).
species.internal_notes: SPECIES ID CONFIRMED (firsthand, Randy): flowers are yellow with black spots and blooming now — confirms true Dietes bicolor. Botanical name correct as listed.

quick_hits: Despite the name, the Screw Pine is no pine at all — it's a monocot, more closely related to grasses, palms, and orchids than to any conifer. The 'screw' comes from the spiral of leaves winding up its trunk.

It walks on stilts: woody prop roots brace the trunk from several feet out, and the trunk flares wide at the base — the park's best specimens, lit up at our holiday show, look uncannily like Hawaiian hula dancers.
Only female trees make the big pineapple-like fruit balls — and ours are fruiting right now, so the heavy globes are out.
Watch your head near the low fruit and mind the saw-toothed leaf edges — these are armed plants.
species.origin: Native to the Mascarene Islands of the Indian Ocean — Mauritius, Rodrigues, and Réunion — and long naturalized in Madagascar, where it was once thought native. One of roughly 750 species of Pandanus (the screwpines), spread across the Old World tropics from Africa to the Pacific.
more_information: The Screw Pine looks like a tree caught mid-stride. Woody prop roots descend from the trunk and lower branches, bracing the plant several feet out from its base, while the trunk itself flares dramatically wide at the bottom. At Palma Sola the finest specimens — four in a row directly behind the office, between the building and the walking path — take full advantage of that silhouette. During the park's winter holiday light show, staff wrap the trunks and every arm in lights, and the flared, root-braced form lit from within looks remarkably like a Hawaiian hula dancer. They're among the show's most striking features.

The common name comes from the way the long leaves spiral around the trunk like the thread of a screw — a pattern that grows more pronounced as the plant ages and old leaf scars ring the bark. The species name utilis means 'useful,' and it has earned it: across the Indian Ocean and Pacific islands, Pandanus leaves are dried, rolled, and woven into mats, baskets, hats, ropes, and thatch — a tradition alive from Kerala to Hawaii.

A word of caution to go with the beauty: those four specimens sit between the office and the walking path rather than on it, but their low fruit can still catch an unwary head, and the leaf edges and tips are genuinely sharp. Admire them, but mind the clearance.
wildlife_value: The Screw Pine's architecture does quiet wildlife work. Its dense spiraling crown and the cage of stilt-like prop roots create sheltered microhabitats at and above ground level, giving cover to lizards, insects, and small animals. The fragrant male flowers draw insects, and the large compound fruits, ripening from green to orange-red, are eaten by mammals — in Florida, squirrels are the most common visitors, gnawing the fibrous segments. Birds use the spiny crown as protected nesting and roosting cover.

blocks.label: Dioecious
blocks.text: male and female flowers grow on separate trees, so only female plants bear fruit. Propagated by seed or by offsets/suckers; prop roots are adventitious, growing from the trunk and branches down into the soil.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Male flowers in long, fragrant, branched clusters surrounded by white bracts; female flowers in rounder, cone-like heads. On separate trees.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Large, pineapple-like multiple fruit (a cluster of fused woody drupes) 6 to 8 inches across, ripening from green to orange-red. Edible but fibrous, and must be cooked; not flavorful to people, but wildlife takes it.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Long, strap-shaped, blue-green, up to 6 feet, spiraling around the trunk; edges and midrib lined with sharp teeth, tapering to a spiny tip.

blocks.label: Trunk and roots
blocks.text: Upright trunk flaring wide at the base, ringed with spiral leaf scars, braced by woody aerial prop roots.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Find the four specimens behind the office and read the form — the wide flared base, the stilt prop roots, the spiral of leaf scars climbing the trunk. Look for the heavy fruit balls on the female trees (out right now), ripening green to orange-red. Picture the silhouette lit at the holiday show — that hula-dancer shape is the whole reason these are featured. And keep a respectful distance from the saw-edged leaves.

size.habit: upright trunk flaring at the base, braced by aerial prop roots, topped by a spiral crown of long sword-like leaves
size.texture: bold, architectural; leaves to 6 feet with sharply toothed edges and a spiny midrib

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sandy, well-drained; wide range
growing_conditions.salt_tolerance: very high — a true coastal plant
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: frost-sensitive; USDA zones 10-11
growing_conditions.note: site away from walking paths — sharp leaves and low fruit are a physical hazard

edibility.detail: Edible with preparation — the fibrous fruit must be cooked and is not especially flavorful to people. Across the Pacific, related Pandanus species are important staple foods. Not a food plant in Florida landscapes.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity. Physical hazard only — the saw-toothed leaf edges, spiny tips, and heavy low-hanging fruit can injure. Use care nearby.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA; no specific toxicity data. The sharp leaves are a physical hazard to pets as to people.

invasive.notes: Not on Florida's FISC invasive lists.

species.other_notes: PARK CONTEXT: Best specimens are four in a row directly behind the office, between the building and the walking path (not on the path). Featured in the winter holiday light show — trunks and arms wrapped in lights; flared, prop-rooted form looks like a Hawaiian hula dancer. Female trees fruiting now (large fruit balls out). HAZARD: low fruit can hit heads; leaf edges/tips are sharp.

quick_hits: That big sky-blue flowering bush near the front entrance is Plumbago — and at Palma Sola it's an unusually large one, anchoring Welcome Island as you come in.
Its flowers are a favorite of the Cassius blue butterfly, whose caterpillars feed on the plant — a small blue butterfly for a sky-blue flower.
Look closely and the flower base is sticky: glandular hairs let the spent blooms cling to fur and clothing, hitchhiking seeds to new ground.
The name comes from the Latin plumbum, 'lead' — it was once believed to cure lead poisoning.
species.origin: Native to South Africa and Mozambique, where it grows as a scrambling shrub in lowland scrub and thicket. Now one of the most popular warm-climate flowering shrubs worldwide, valued for its near year-round clouds of pale blue bloom.
more_information: Plumbago is grown almost everywhere warm for one reason: few shrubs produce this much soft sky-blue flower for so little effort. It blooms spring through fall (nearly year-round in Florida), shrugs off drought once established, and resprouts cheerfully after hard pruning — which it needs, because left alone it becomes a sprawling, scrambling beast.

At Palma Sola there isn't much of it — it's mostly just the one specimen, but it's a memorable one: a large blue-flowered bush on Welcome Island near the front entrance, right beside the Tree Crinum, and notably big for a plumbago. It takes regular cutting and trimming to keep in bounds. The spot has a small sad history, too — the plumbago once grew around a Bismarck palm that was lost in the hurricanes, one of several specimens the storms took from the park.

Two nice details reward a close look. The flower's name traces to the Latin plumbum, 'lead': the plant was once thought to cure lead poisoning (and 'leadwort' carries the same idea). And the base of each flower is coated in sticky glandular hairs — sticky enough that in its native South Africa, children press the blossoms onto their ears like little earrings. That same stickiness is the plant's seed-dispersal trick, hitching spent flower capsules onto passing fur and clothing.
wildlife_value: Plumbago is a genuine butterfly magnet. It is the larval host plant for the Cassius blue (Leptotes cassius), a tiny native blue butterfly whose caterpillars feed on the foliage and flowers, and its nectar-rich, long-tubed blue flowers draw butterflies, long-tongued bees, and other pollinators through most of the year. The sticky seed capsules enlist passing animals for dispersal. As a large, dense, evergreen shrub, it also offers nesting and shelter cover for small birds — useful structure right at the park's entrance.

blocks.label: Reproduction
blocks.text: Spreads by underground runners (suckers) and seed; propagated easily from seed, cuttings, or rooted suckers. Vigorous — resprouts readily after heavy pruning.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Dense terminal clusters of five-petaled, phlox-like flowers about an inch wide, pale sky-blue (white and deeper-blue forms exist), each on a slender tube. Blooms spring through fall, nearly year-round in Florida.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: A small, barbed, sticky capsule — the glandular-haired calyx clings to fur and clothing, dispersing the seed.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Thin, oval, bright green (greyish beneath), to about 2 inches, arranged alternately on arching semi-woody stems.

blocks.label: Habit
blocks.text: Mounding to scrambling shrub; can climb or sprawl several feet if unpruned.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: At the front entrance, find the big blue-flowered bush on Welcome Island beside the Tree Crinum. Touch a spent flower cluster — feel how it sticks to your fingertip; that's the seed-dispersal mechanism at work. Watch the blooms for small blue butterflies (the Cassius blue) darting around their host plant.

size.height: typically 3 to 6 feet (to 10+ if left to scramble); the park's specimen is large for the species
size.habit: mounding, arching, scrambling semi-woody shrub
size.texture: fine to medium; soft mounding form

growing_conditions.light: full sun to partial shade (best bloom in sun)
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: wide range, well-drained; tolerates poor and saline soils
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: frost-sensitive tops; resprouts from base; USDA zones 9-11
growing_conditions.note: vigorous and sprawling — needs regular pruning to stay in bounds; wear gloves (sap irritates skin)

edibility.detail: Not edible. Toxic if ingested.

toxicity.people: Mildly toxic. The sap can irritate skin, and ingestion can cause digestive upset and mouth/throat irritation. Wear gloves when pruning. Not a serious hazard, but not for eating.
toxicity.dogs: Mildly toxic to dogs if eaten — can cause drooling, vomiting, and mouth irritation. Animals usually avoid it; discourage chewing and keep clippings away from pets.

invasive.notes: Not on Florida's FISC invasive lists. Spreads by suckers and sticky seed and naturalizes in places, so it's vigorous and needs containment, but it is not a listed invasive.

species.other_notes: PARK CONTEXT: One main specimen, on Welcome Island near the front entrance, beside the Tree Crinum — large for a plumbago and needs regular trimming to control its sprawl. Little else of it elsewhere in the park. It formerly grew around a Bismarck palm that was lost in the hurricanes.

quick_hits: Snake Plants are sprinkled all around the park in many shapes and sizes — spotting them in their variety makes a fun scavenger hunt as you walk.
Famous as the nearly indestructible houseplant, it survives deep shade, drought, and neglect that would kill almost anything else.
For years it was the well-known Sansevieria; DNA evidence in 2017 moved it into Dracaena, the genus of the dragon trees and lucky bamboo.
There's a dedicated patch just past the front gate planted by Bright Futures high school volunteers — for some, the very first plant they learned to recognize.
species.origin: Native to tropical West Africa, from Nigeria east to the Congo, where it grows in dry, rocky ground. Now one of the most widely grown houseplants on Earth, kept in nearly every climate for its toughness and striking upright form.
more_information: The Snake Plant is the plant that refuses to die. It tolerates deep shade or bright sun, goes weeks without water, and asks for almost nothing — which is why it's a fixture of offices, lobbies, and first-time plant owners' windowsills worldwide. Its stiff, banded, sword-shaped leaves give it its other old name, mother-in-law's tongue, while the tough leaf fibers earned it 'viper's bowstring hemp' — they were genuinely strong enough to string bows.

It also carries a famous reputation worth handling honestly. NASA's 1989 Clean Air Study found the Snake Plant removed several common indoor pollutants (formaldehyde, benzene, and others) in a sealed laboratory chamber. That made it a marketing legend as an 'air-purifying plant' — but later analysis showed the filtration rate is far too slow to meaningfully clean the air of a real room; you'd need dozens or hundreds of plants per room to matter. It's a wonderful, resilient plant; just not a working air filter.

A note on its name: for decades this was Sansevieria trifasciata, a name still everywhere in the nursery trade. In 2017, DNA studies showed Sansevieria was nested within the genus Dracaena, so it was reclassified as Dracaena trifasciata — placing the humble snake plant among the dragon trees and lucky bamboo.

At Palma Sola, Snake Plants turn up all over in different forms and sizes, so they make a good spot-them-as-you-go exercise. One patch has a story: just past the front gate, down the footpath to the left and beyond the big Staghorn Fern on the right, Bright Futures high school volunteers spent a couple of hours planting out extra nursery stock. The patch is regularly maintained, and for several students it was the first plant they ever learned to identify — some have since reported back that now they see snake plants everywhere they go. That's the Baader-Meinhof phenomenon (the 'frequency illusion'): once your brain learns a pattern, it starts noticing it constantly. A first plant well learned is a small superpower.
wildlife_value: As a non-native succulent, the Snake Plant isn't a major wildlife plant, but it isn't idle either. When it blooms — usually after a spell of stress — it sends up tall spikes of greenish-white tubular flowers that are strongly fragrant at night and rich in nectar, drawing moths and other night pollinators. Its dense, spreading clumps of stiff leaves give ground-level cover to lizards and insects. Outdoors in a mild climate like Florida's, those same vigorous rhizomes mean it can spread into colonies, so its modest wildlife value comes paired with a need to keep it contained.

blocks.label: Reproduction
blocks.text: Spreads steadily by creeping underground rhizomes, forming dense colonies; also propagated by division or leaf cuttings. (Leaf cuttings lose the yellow leaf-edge variegation — only division keeps it.)

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Occasional tall spikes of slender greenish-white tubular flowers, strongly night-fragrant, attracting moths. Blooming is sporadic and often follows stress.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Stiff, upright, sword-shaped, dark green with paler gray-green cross-banding; many forms have yellow leaf margins. The banding suggests snakeskin — the source of the common name.

blocks.label: Habit
blocks.text: Upright, architectural, clump-forming, slowly expanding outward by rhizome.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: As you walk the park, play spot-the-snake-plant — note how much they vary in size, leaf width, and the amount of yellow edging. The student-planted patch is just past the front gate: take the footpath to the left, go beyond the big Staghorn Fern on the right, and you'll find it. Look for the snakeskin cross-banding on the stiff upright leaves, and if you're lucky, a fragrant flower spike.

size.height: 2 to 4 feet for most forms (dwarf and taller types exist)
size.spread: clumping, slowly spreading by underground rhizomes
size.habit: upright, architectural, stiff sword-like leaves
size.texture: bold, vertical; leaves banded dark and light green, often yellow-margined

growing_conditions.light: deep shade to full sun — exceptionally adaptable
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: any well-drained soil; actually prefers poor, dry ground
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: damaged below ~50°F for extended periods; USDA zones 9-11 outdoors
growing_conditions.note: nearly indestructible; main risk is overwatering/rot. Spreads by rhizome — contain it outdoors

edibility.detail: Not edible. Contains saponins that are toxic if ingested.

toxicity.people: Mildly toxic. Contains saponins; ingestion causes nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea, and the sap can mildly irritate skin. Symptoms are usually mild, but keep children from chewing it.
toxicity.dogs: Toxic to dogs (ASPCA-listed). The saponins cause drooling, vomiting, and diarrhea if chewed or eaten. Usually mild and self-limiting, but discourage pets from chewing the leaves.

invasive.notes: Not on Florida's FISC invasive lists, but a vigorous rhizome-spreader that can form colonies and naturalize in warm climates (considered a potential weed in parts of Australia). Worth containing outdoors — monitor.

species.other_notes:
NOMENCLATURE: Formerly Sansevieria trifasciata, the universally known name for decades. Reclassified to Dracaena trifasciata in 2017 after molecular studies showed Sansevieria was nested within Dracaena; under naming rules the older genus name (Dracaena, 1767) takes precedence. Family Asparagaceae. 'Sansevieria' is still common in the nursery trade.

PARK CONTEXT: Snake plants sprinkled throughout the park in varied forms/sizes (good spot-them exercise). Dedicated patch planted by Bright Futures high school volunteers: just past the front gate, down the footpath to the LEFT, beyond the big Staghorn Fern on the right. Regularly maintained; for several students it was the first plant they learned to ID. Tie-in: Baader-Meinhof / frequency illusion (once learned, seen everywhere).

quick_hits: Grows on trees, not soil — this entire colony is clinging to a palm trunk the way its ancestors clung to rainforest canopy.
Two completely different leaf types on the same plant: flat brown shield fronds grip the host and trap falling debris as compost; arching antler-like fronds handle photosynthesis.
Ferns predate flowering plants by over 200 million years. This one reproduces by releasing microscopic spores — no flowers, no fruit, no seeds.
The specimens growing at ground level throughout the park are donations — some arrive large enough to require a tractor to move.
species.origin: Non-native. Native to coastal Queensland and New South Wales, Australia, Bali, Java, and New Guinea. Among the most cold-tolerant staghorn ferns — survives brief temperatures down to 30°F. Member of Polypodiaceae.
more_information: Staghorn ferns sit near the base of the land-plant family tree — ferns evolved over 200 million years before the first flowering plant appeared.

This colony is growing terrestrially, wrapped around a palm base rather than suspended in a tree canopy. That is not typical, but it is documented. Research on Platycerium bifurcatum shows that colonies dislodged from trees can reestablish on the ground, and the plants reorganize themselves accordingly.

The two frond types have distinct jobs. Shield fronds are flat, papery, and brown — they grip the host surface and trap falling leaf litter, slowly decomposing it into nutrients the plant absorbs through its root mass.
