blocks.text: Gray-green with large conical spines on trunk and major branches — same defensive strategy as the adjacent Kapok. Develops buttressing at the base with age.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: In late winter, the red flowers on bare branches are visible from across the park. Year-round, the spiny trunk and the proximity to the Kapok and Baobab make this easy to locate. Compare trunk spines between the Red Silk Cotton and the Kapok side by side.

size.height_length: a large deciduous tree, commonly 40 to 80 feet in cultivation and taller in its native range
size.habit: tall, straight-trunked tree, the young trunk armed with stout conical spines; tiered branches
size.texture: bold - large leaves, big red flowers, stout spines

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: deep, well-drained soils; tolerates a range
growing_conditions.drought_tolerance: high once established; drops its leaves in the dry season
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: frost-tender; USDA zones 10 to 11
growing_conditions.note: flowers on bare branches in winter to early spring before the leaves return; produces capsules of silky, kapok-like fiber.

edibility.detail: Seeds are edible when properly processed. Flowers edible in some traditional preparations. Not a significant food plant in Florida landscapes.

toxicity.people: No significant toxicity to people documented.
toxicity.dogs: No significant toxicity to dogs documented.

invasive.notes: Not on Florida's FISC invasive plant list. Not flagged by UF/IFAS.

quick_hits: A Florida native in the coffee family (Rubiaceae) — same family as coffee, Pentas, Firebush, and Ixora in the park collection — but not a source of true coffee. The small red fruits do contain caffeine in trace amounts, which is a family trait.
One of the best native shrubs for Florida butterfly and wildlife gardens. The berries are eaten by at least 20 bird species. The flowers attract native bees and butterflies.
Tolerates deep shade, which is unusual for a fruiting shrub. One of the few native plants that performs well under oak canopy.
The glossy, deeply veined dark leaves are distinctive and attractive year-round.

Early Florida pioneers occasionally roasted the berries as a coffee substitute. They quickly abandoned the practice — the resulting drink was notoriously bitter and caused severe headaches. The seeds are best left for the birds.
species.origin: Native to South and Central Florida, the Caribbean, and Central America. Found naturally in hammock understory under live oak and other hardwoods. Member of Rubiaceae — the coffee family.
more_information: At Palma Sola, Wild Coffee grows in the tangle between the gazebo and Palm Alley, tucked among the crotons. It shares that area with the Wild Lime — watch your step around those thorns. The spot is a bit wild back there, which suits it perfectly.
wildlife_value: One of the most wildlife-productive native shrubs for Central and South Florida. At least 20 bird species eat the berries, including catbirds, mockingbirds, and thrushes. The flowers attract native bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds.
An important understory plant in the hammock community — tolerates the shade conditions under which most flowering shrubs fail.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, white, tubular, in clusters. Produced in spring and summer.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Small, round, red ripening to black. Two-seeded. Produced in clusters. Edible but not significant as a food plant.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Opposite, glossy dark green, with deeply impressed veins giving a quilted or corrugated appearance. This is the year-round ID feature.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Evergreen shrub, 4 to 15 feet, depending on light conditions. Shade-tolerant.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: The deeply veined, glossy, corrugated-looking dark green leaves are distinctive year-round. No other common native Florida shrub has quite this leaf texture. Red to black berry clusters when fruiting.

size.height_length: 4 to 6 feet typically; can reach 8 to 10 feet in shade
size.habit: rounded evergreen shrub with glossy, deeply veined leaves

growing_conditions.light: part shade to full shade preferred; tolerates more sun with paler leaves
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: well-drained, organic to sandy; hammock-understory soils
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: damaged by hard freezes; USDA zones 9B to 11
growing_conditions.note: leaves can yellow and burn in full sun; best as an understory shrub. A Florida-friendly native for shade.

edibility.detail: Berries technically edible but not significant as a food plant. Contain trace caffeine — a family characteristic. Not typically consumed.

toxicity.people: No significant toxicity to people.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs.

invasive.notes: Florida native. Not invasive.

species.category: Plants to Watch and Invasive Awareness
quick_hits: One of the most recognizable groundcovers in Florida — the yellow daisy flowers and creeping mat habit are everywhere in landscaping, medians, and disturbed areas.
FISC Category I invasive in Florida. Spreads into natural areas via runners and displaces native groundcovers in coastal hammocks and beach strand communities.
Kept here as an educational specimen — a demonstration that familiar and widely planted plants can be among the most ecologically damaging.
The park actively manages its spread.

This is the plant our volunteers battle most. It covers nearly every corner of the park and we manage it entirely without chemicals — by hand, one runner at a time. If you see a volunteer on their knees pulling yellow daisies, now you know why. We could use the help.
species.origin: Non-native. Native to Mexico, Central America, and the Caribbean. Naturalized and invasive throughout the tropical world. Member of Asteraceae — the daisy family.
more_information: Wedelia is one of the most recognizable groundcovers in Florida - glossy three-lobed leaves and bright yellow daisy flowers covering medians, slopes, and disturbed ground year-round. It was introduced in the early 1900s from tropical America as an easy ornamental groundcover, and that ease is the problem: it forms dense mats that crowd out and shade other plants.

It spreads almost entirely vegetatively - stems root wherever they touch soil, and broken fragments start new plants - which is why mowing or careless dumping of clippings only helps it move. It seldom relies on seed. Tough enough to shrug off drought, salt, freezes, and heavy shade, it is listed by FISC as a Category II invasive and is on the IUCN list of the world's 100 worst invasive species.

It is easy to confuse with the park's native Beach Sunflower (PSBP-00008), which carries similar yellow daisy flowers - a useful side-by-side lesson in telling a well-behaved native from an aggressive look-alike. Palma Sola keeps Wedelia as a contained, managed educational specimen.
wildlife_value: The yellow flowers draw bees and butterflies, but the plant's wildlife value is outweighed by its harm: by forming dense single-species mats it displaces the native plants that support far more insects and wildlife. Its ecological cost is the lesson it teaches here.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Bright yellow to yellow-orange, daisy-like, about 1 inch across, held singly on short stalks above the foliage. Nearly year-round.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Fleshy, glossy, dark green, usually three-lobed, 2 to 4 inches long, with toothed margins.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Low, mat-forming perennial; rounded stems root at the nodes as they creep.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A dense mat of glossy three-lobed leaves studded with yellow daisies. Compare with the native Beach Sunflower - similar flower, very different plant and behavior.

size.height_length: about 8 to 12 inches tall as a mat
size.habit: mat-forming perennial groundcover

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: nearly any; dry to wet, including poor sandy and waterlogged soils
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: may freeze back but recovers; USDA zones 8B to 11
growing_conditions.note: a FISC Category II invasive - do not plant; remove by the roots; do not mow, which spreads fragments.

edibility.detail: Non-toxic. Not a food plant.

toxicity.people: Not significantly toxic to people.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs.

invasive.notes: FISC Category II invasive in Florida, and on the IUCN list of the world's 100 worst invasive species. Spreads aggressively by runners and stem fragments rather than seed, forming dense mats that crowd out other plants. Introduced in the early 1900s as an ornamental groundcover; do not plant, and remove existing plants by the roots. Avoid mowing, which fragments and spreads it.

species.category: Plants to Watch and Invasive Awareness
quick_hits: A low-growing groundcover with cheerful white to pale-pink, star-shaped flowers, often dismissed as a lawn weed. It belongs to the coffee family (Rubiaceae) and is widely called Florida snow for the way its blooms can cover a lawn, and Mexican clover.

Though non-native - it came from Brazil and naturalized across Florida in the 20th century - it is a genuinely good nectar plant for bees and butterflies, which is why some pollinator gardeners tolerate or even welcome it.
species.origin: Non-native. Native to South America, primarily Brazil. Naturalized across Florida in the 20th century. Member of Rubiaceae — the coffee family, same as Firebush, Wild Coffee, and Pentas in the collection.
more_information: Largeflower Pusley is one of the plants behind the nickname Florida snow - in fall and winter its small white-to-pink flowers can blanket a thin lawn. It is a creeping perennial in the coffee family (Rubiaceae), the same family as the park's Wild Coffee and as commercial coffee and gardenias, and it is native to Brazil, naturalized across central and southern Florida over the last century.

Turf managers treat it as a persistent weed, but it sits in the love-hate category: its flowers are a reliable nectar source for bees and butterflies, and some pollinator gardeners leave it in place for that reason. It thrives where turf is thin, soils are sandy, or lawns are stressed, spreading by seed and by stems that root at the nodes.
It is recorded here as part of documenting everything growing on the grounds - a common, pretty, non-native groundcover most people have seen but never named.
wildlife_value: A useful nectar source for bees and butterflies over a long blooming season - its main redeeming wildlife value. It offers little else, and as a non-native it does not host native insects the way a native groundcover would.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, star-shaped, white to pink or lavender, about a third of an inch across, clustered at the branch tips. Long blooming, heaviest fall through winter.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Opposite, hairy, narrowly elliptical, tapering at both ends.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Low, creeping perennial with hairy branching stems that root at the nodes, forming mats.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Mats of small star-shaped white-to-pink flowers in thin turf and sandy disturbed ground, busiest with small pollinators in the cooler months.

size.habit: creeping perennial groundcover

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sandy, disturbed soils and thin turf; tolerates poor ground
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: perennial in warm areas; USDA zones 9 to 11
growing_conditions.note: a non-native lawn weed; spreads by seed and stem fragments. Mowing before seed-set limits spread.

edibility.detail: Non-toxic. Not a food plant.

toxicity.people: Not toxic.
toxicity.dogs: Not toxic to dogs.

invasive.notes: Non-native, naturalized from South America. Not on Florida's FISC invasive list. A common lawn and turf weed in central and southern Florida; spreads by seed and stem fragments.

species.category: Plants to Watch and Invasive Awareness
quick_hits: A tiny orchid that grows as a lawn weed throughout warm-climate Florida — visible as a small rosette of flat leaves followed by a thin spike of inconspicuous white flowers.

Not native to Florida. Introduced from Asia, it naturalized quietly in Florida lawns and is now ubiquitous in mowed areas. Because it is small and flowers between mowings, most people never notice it.
Documented here as part of the honest inventory. It is present at the park as a naturalized weed in mowed areas and is technically an orchid — one of the smallest and most unassuming in the family.

An evolutionary speedster — in Florida it has adapted to residential mowing schedules. It can shoot up a flower spike, get pollinated, and drop its seeds all within a single 7 to 10 day window between lawn cuts.
A true orchid (Orchidaceae) hiding in the turf grass. The smallest and most overlooked member of one of the largest plant families on earth.
species.origin: Non-native. Native to tropical Asia. Naturalized in Florida and other warm-climate states in disturbed grassy areas. Member of Orchidaceae — the orchid family.
more_information: The Lawn Orchid is a genuine orchid that lives as a lawn weed - one of the small surprises a close look at the ground can turn up. Native to tropical Asia, it was first recorded in the United States near Fellsmere, Florida, in 1936, apparently arriving with imported centipede grass. It spread only once its required soil fungus partner became established locally, since orchids depend on such fungi to germinate.

It appears in cool, moist months as a small rosette of narrow leaves, then sends up a short spike of tiny white flowers with a yellow lip, and is gone again - the species name strateumatica comes from a Greek word for a marching band of soldiers, fitting a plant that turns up in ranks and then vanishes. It largely sets seed by self-pollination, which is why it can colonize new lawns on its own.
It is harmless and not a management concern; it is recorded here as a small botanical curiosity hiding in plain sight.
wildlife_value: Little direct wildlife value. The flowers are small and largely self-pollinating, so they support few visitors. Its interest is botanical - a wild orchid completing its life cycle in mowed turf.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Tiny, white with a yellow lip, in a short compact spike a few inches tall, mainly in winter and early spring.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Narrow, grass-like to lance-shaped, in a small low rosette, often with a slight reddish tinge.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A small terrestrial orchid, only a few inches tall, appearing seasonally in lawns and damp open turf.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: In winter, scan short grass for a small rosette topped by a stubby spike of little white-and-yellow flowers. Easy to miss and easy to mow over.

size.height_length: about 2 to 8 inches tall in flower
size.spread: a small rosette a few inches across
size.habit: low seasonal terrestrial orchid

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: moist, grassy, disturbed ground; lawns and turf
growing_conditions.drought_tolerance: low - appears in the cool, moist season
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: appears in winter and early spring; USDA zones 9 to 11
growing_conditions.note: depends on a soil fungus partner; appears and disappears seasonally. Harmless; no control needed.

edibility.detail: Non-toxic. Not a food plant.

toxicity.people: Not toxic.
toxicity.dogs: Not toxic to dogs.

invasive.notes: Naturalized in Florida lawns. Not on the FISC invasive list. Not a significant ecological concern.

species.category: Plants to Watch and Invasive Awareness
quick_hits: The seeds rattle in the dry pods — hence the name. As the bean-like pods mature they inflate like small balloons and the loose seeds inside turn the plant into a natural maraca. A delightful sound for something so toxic.
A persistent volunteer to the right of the office steps. It owns that spot and keeps coming back. One day something nicer will go there.
species.origin: Non-native. Native to tropical Asia and Africa. Naturalized throughout Florida's disturbed areas. Member of Fabaceae — the legume family.
more_information: Smooth Rattlebox is a non-native legume from the Old World tropics that has naturalized widely across Florida's disturbed ground, roadsides, and open fields. Like other rattleboxes, its dry seed pods inflate as they mature, and the loose seeds inside rattle when shaken - the source of the name and of the genus name Crotalaria, from the Greek for a rattle.

The whole plant contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids, toxic to birds, livestock, and people if eaten, and responsible for poisoning in grazing animals. One insect turns that chemistry to its advantage: the Ornate Bella Moth (Utetheisa ornatrix), a showy day-flying moth whose caterpillars feed on Crotalaria, take up the alkaloids, and carry them into adulthood as a defense against predators. The caterpillars often destroy the seeds as they feed, a small check on a weedy plant.
It is a FISC Category II invasive in Florida and spreads readily in the sandy, disturbed soils common around Manatee County.
wildlife_value: The flowers are visited by bees and butterflies, but the plant's notable wildlife role is as a larval host for the Ornate Bella Moth (Utetheisa ornatrix), one of Florida's few showy day-flying moths. The caterpillars sequester the plant's toxic alkaloids for their own protection and often consume the developing seeds. The toxic foliage and seeds otherwise deter most browsing animals.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Yellow, pea-shaped, in upright clusters (racemes) at the stem tips, often streaked with fine reddish-brown lines.

blocks.label: Pods and Seeds
blocks.text: Green ripening to brown, swollen and inflated; the dry seeds rattle inside. The ID confirmation.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Compound, each with three oval leaflets (trifoliate).

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Erect, branching annual to short-lived perennial herb, woody at the base.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Upright stems of yellow pea flowers above three-part leaves, followed by inflated pods that rattle when shaken. Look for the brightly colored Bella Moth or its caterpillars on the plant.

size.habit: erect, branching annual to short-lived perennial herb, woody at the base

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sandy, well-drained, disturbed soils; tolerates poor ground (fixes nitrogen)
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: dies back in frost and reseeds; USDA zones 9 to 11
growing_conditions.note: a FISC Category II invasive; all parts toxic. Remove volunteers; handle seeds and pods with care.

edibility.detail: Not edible. Toxic.

toxicity.people: Toxic. Contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids causing liver damage in humans and animals if ingested. Do not eat any part of this plant.
toxicity.dogs: Toxic to dogs. Pyrrolizidine alkaloids cause progressive liver damage. Keep pets away from pods and seeds.

invasive.notes: FISC Category II — spreads aggressively in sandy disturbed coastal soils, roadsides, and open fields common to Manatee County. invasive in Florida. Naturalized in disturbed areas. Remove volunteers promptly.

quick_hits: Not related to orchids. The common name comes from the flower shape, which bears a passing resemblance. The actual family is Fabaceae — the legume family — making it a relative of beans, peas, and the park's own Royal Poinciana.

The two-lobed leaf — perfectly split at the tip — is the identification key. Each leaf looks like a butterfly, or like a camel's footprint. The genus name Bauhinia honors twin botanists, the brothers Bauhin — the two-lobed leaf being a botanical joke about the twin namesakes.
One of the most abundant street trees in Hong Kong, where it is also the emblem on the city's flag. The flag version is the sterile hybrid B. × blakeana, which does not produce seed pods.
Blooms heavily in late winter when little else is flowering.

The specimen that catches most visitors' eyes grows just beyond our parking lot fence on adjacent county property — visible, admired, and not yet ours. We are keeping an eye out for one to add to the collection proper. If you would like to donate an Orchid Tree to the park, we would know exactly where to put it.
species.origin: Non-native. Native to southern China, India, and Southeast Asia. Member of Fabaceae — the legume family. Widely planted as a street and garden tree throughout the tropics and subtropics.
more_information: The Orchid Tree blooms in late winter to early spring — a period when the park's flowering calendar is relatively quiet — which gives it an outsized visual impact for its size. The flowers are lavender-pink to purple, up to 4 inches across, covering the branches in a display that is visible from a distance.

The two-lobed leaf is a reliable year-round ID feature. Each leaf is completely divided at the tip into two rounded lobes, giving it the outline of a butterfly or a camel track. The genus Bauhinia was named in 1694 by the botanist Plumier to honor the twin brothers Jean and Gaspard Bauhin, prominent 16th-century botanists — the two-lobed leaf being the botanical equivalent of a naming pun.
The park's own Pink Orchid Tree (Bauhinia monandra, PSBP-00114) grows in a separate location — find it in the southeast corner past the bridge from the fruit garden.
wildlife_value: Butterflies, bees, and hummingbirds visit the flowers. The late-winter bloom timing provides nectar at a period of relative scarcity for pollinators.
Seed pods are produced abundantly after flowering — long, flat, woody legume pods that twist and split explosively to disperse seeds. Birds feed on the seeds.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Lavender-pink to purple, with five petals, one marked with a deeper color stripe. Up to 4 inches across. Produced in late winter to early spring, often before the tree fully leafs out.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Long, flat, woody legume pods, 8 to 12 inches long. Twist and split explosively at maturity to disperse seeds. Produced in abundance.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: The defining feature: two-lobed, each lobe rounded, split at the tip. The outline resembles a butterfly or a camel's footprint. Medium green, smooth.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Semi-deciduous small to medium tree, typically 20 to 35 feet. Open, spreading canopy.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: The two-lobed leaves are the reliable year-round marker — no other common Florida tree has this leaf shape. In late winter, the lavender-pink flowers are distinctive. Compare with the Pink Orchid Tree (B. monandra, PSBP-00114) elsewhere in the collection — smaller flowers, single fertile stamen vs. the multiple stamens of B. variegata.

size.habit: small to medium flowering tree, often multi-trunked

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: wide range, well-drained
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: frost-tender; USDA zones 9B to 11
growing_conditions.note: drops leaves in winter around bloom time; seed pods split explosively and self-seed, so remove volunteers.

edibility.detail: Young leaves and flowers edible in some Asian cuisines — cooked as a vegetable. Flower buds used in pickles. Not a standard food plant in Florida landscape use.

toxicity.people: No significant toxicity to people.
toxicity.dogs: No significant toxicity to dogs documented.

invasive.notes: Not on Florida's FISC invasive plant list. Self-seeds freely — seed pods explosive at maturity. Monitor for volunteers in adjoining natural areas.

quick_hits: A native creeping groundcover that grows in lawns, path edges, and disturbed moist ground throughout Florida. The tiny white flower clusters are insignificant to the human eye but are a documented nectar source for small native bees and the larval host for several butterfly species including the White Peacock and Phaon Crescent.
Tolerates foot traffic and mowing. Forms a flat mat.
Primarily documented as part of the naturalized park ground flora.
You'll usually spot these tiny underdogs along the compacted edges of walking paths or tucked into mulch beds after a summer rain. Our volunteers mostly leave them alone unless they get too rowdy.
species.origin: Native to Florida and the Americas generally, from the eastern United States through Central and South America. Found in lawns, path edges, and moist disturbed ground. Member of Verbenaceae — the verbena family.
more_information: Turkey Tangle Frogfruit is a tough native groundcover that most Floridians have walked past in lawns, path edges, and damp open ground without giving it a name. It forms a low, dense mat of small green leaves studded with tiny flower heads - a white ring of florets around a small dark center, like a matchhead, which gives it another of its common names.

For a plant this small, its wildlife value is large. It is a nectar source for bees and many small butterflies and a larval host for three butterflies in particular - the White Peacock, the Phaon Crescent, and the Common Buckeye. The Phaon Crescent depends almost entirely on frogfruit and its close relatives to complete its life cycle.

It tolerates drought, flooding, foot traffic, mowing, compacted soil, and salt, which is why it is increasingly planted as a low-water lawn alternative and for erosion control. At Palma Sola it does the quiet work of covering ground and feeding pollinators close to the soil.
wildlife_value: A documented larval host for the White Peacock and Phaon Crescent butterflies. A useful nectar plant for small native bees despite the tiny flower size. One of the butterflies' host plants that is genuinely inconspicuous — visitors rarely notice it but it supports several common Florida butterfly species.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Tiny, in a compact head: a ring of white to pale-purple florets around a small dark center, on a slender stalk held just above the foliage. Blooms over a long warm season.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: A tiny dry two-parted fruit; the plant also spreads readily by creeping stems that root at the nodes.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Small, opposite, ovate to spatulate, with a few teeth toward the tip.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Low, mat-forming, creeping groundcover rooting as it spreads.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A flat green mat in damp or open ground with tiny matchhead-like flowers on short stalks. Look for small butterflies working the blooms low to the ground.

size.spread: 8 to 10 feet or more as it creeps and roots
size.habit: low, mat-forming, evergreen creeping groundcover

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: any texture; well-drained to seasonally wet; tolerates compacted soil
growing_conditions.drought_tolerance: moderate to high; also tolerates flooding
growing_conditions.salt_tolerance: moderate to high - tolerates salt wind and occasional salt flooding
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: evergreen in warm winters, root-hardy; USDA zones 8 to 11
growing_conditions.note: takes light foot traffic and mowing; avoid mowing while in bloom. A native lawn alternative.

edibility.detail: Not an edible plant.

toxicity.people: Not toxic.
toxicity.dogs: Not toxic to dogs.

invasive.notes: Florida native. Not invasive.

quick_hits: A Florida native coastal shrub that most visitors have seen along roadsides and waterways without knowing its name. The late-fall fluffy white seed heads — covering female plants in cotton-like masses — are how most people first notice it.
Dioecious — male and female flowers on separate plants. Only female plants produce the showy white seed masses.

A critical late-season nectar source: one of the last native plants to flower before winter, often still blooming in November when almost nothing else is. Critical for migrating monarch butterflies and late-season bees.
Tolerates salt spray, flooding, and brackish conditions that would kill most shrubs.
species.origin: Native to the Atlantic and Gulf coasts of the eastern United States, from Massachusetts to Texas including all of Florida. Found in coastal marshes, mangrove edges, dunes, and disturbed coastal habitats. Member of Asteraceae — the daisy family.
more_information: The Saltbush at Palma Sola is part of the refurbished butterfly garden, brought back to life in 2026 after years of neglect. The garden was cleared of weeds, replanted with native species, and given a new trellis designed and built by a volunteer named Bill — complete with abstract butterfly artwork of his own design. The butterfly garden team maintains it with regular planting, puddle-watering stations, and stone landing spots. The area has been officially designated as a Monarch Waystation in the national registry, complete with a sign — a meaningful recognition of the team's work.
wildlife_value: One of the most important late-season nectar sources on the Florida coast. Flowers in October-November when most nectar sources have finished, providing critical fuel for southward-migrating Monarch butterflies and late-season bees.

Female plants produce massive seed masses eaten by warblers, sparrows, and other small birds during fall migration. The timing is not coincidental — seed production aligns with peak fall songbird migration through Florida.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, white, in clusters. Male and female flowers on separate plants. Female flowers produce the showy fluffy white seed masses in late fall.

blocks.label: Seed heads
blocks.text: The most recognizable feature: masses of white silky fibers on female plants in October-November, covering the plant like cotton.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Alternate, leathery, waxy green, with irregular toothed margins.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Evergreen to semi-evergreen shrub, 6 to 12 feet, fast growing.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: In October-November, female plants covered in white fluffy seed masses are visible from a distance. At other times, the leathery waxy alternate leaves and the coastal setting are the markers.

size.habit: multi-stemmed, somewhat open deciduous to semi-evergreen shrub

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: wide range; wet to dry; brackish and salt-influenced coastal soils
growing_conditions.salt_tolerance: very high - grows along salt marsh and estuary edges
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: excellent; USDA zones 5 to 10
growing_conditions.note: female plants produce showy white seed plumes in fall; wind-dispersed seed means volunteers can appear nearby.

edibility.detail: Not an edible plant.

toxicity.people: No significant toxicity to people.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs.

invasive.notes: Florida native. Not invasive.

quick_hits: One of the most productive fall nectar plants for Florida pollinators. The yellow flower plumes in October and November arrive precisely when most other flowering plants are finishing for the year, providing a critical late-season nectar source.

Goldenrod is falsely blamed for hay fever. The pollen is heavy and sticky — dispersed by insects, not wind. The actual culprit for fall allergies is ragweed, which flowers at the same time but goes unnoticed while goldenrod is conspicuous.
A Florida native found on coastal dunes and shell mounds from Central Florida southward. More salt and drought tolerant than most goldenrods.
Distinct from the Northern Goldenrod varieties — this is a genuinely tropical-coast species.
species.origin: Native to Florida's Atlantic and Gulf coasts and adjacent inland areas, with range extending into the Caribbean. Found on coastal dunes, shell mounds, and disturbed coastal uplands. Member of Asteraceae — the daisy family.
more_information: This goldenrod was spotted in full bloom by an iNaturalist observer and welcomed into the official collection — a community sighting that made it official. It is a recent addition to the butterfly garden, which was refurbished in 2026 and officially designated as a Monarch Waystation in the national registry. In late October and November, watch for migrating Monarchs working the flower plumes as they head south.
wildlife_value: One of the highest-value fall pollinator plants in the Florida coastal palette. Monarchs on southward migration use the flowers as a critical fuel stop. Bees, wasps, beetles, and other insects work the flower plumes intensively during the brief bloom period.
Seed heads eaten by finches and sparrows after flowering. The erect seed heads persist through winter providing structure and food.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Tiny yellow composite blooms in arching plumes, October-November. The plumes are the visual signature.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Alternate, lance-shaped, somewhat leathery. Lower leaves larger, reducing up the stem.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Upright perennial, 2 to 5 feet, spreading by rhizomes to form colonies.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: The arching yellow flower plumes in October-November are unmistakable in their season. Year-round, the upright lance-leaved stems in a spreading colony near the coast.

size.habit: upright perennial wildflower with arching flower plumes

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sandy, well-drained coastal soils; tolerates poor soils and periodic moisture
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: excellent; USDA zones 8 to 11
growing_conditions.note: a fall bloomer; spreads by rhizome, so give it room or divide as needed.

edibility.detail: Not an edible plant.

toxicity.people: Not toxic to people.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs.

invasive.notes: Florida native. Not invasive.

quick_hits: The deeply dissected leaves and coral-red flowers create an unusual texture in the tropical garden — fine-cut foliage on a shrub that otherwise has a bold tropical presence.
A member of Euphorbiaceae — the spurge family — same family as the Copperleaf, Chenille Plant, and Pencil Tree in the park collection. The milky sap is the family signature.
The coral-red tubular flowers attract hummingbirds. The distinctive coral-red star-shaped fruit capsules that follow are nearly as ornamental as the flowers.
All parts toxic — the euphorbiacea sap and seed compounds are the concern.
species.origin: Non-native. Native to tropical Mexico and Central America. Member of Euphorbiaceae — the spurge family.
more_information: At Palma Sola the Coral Plant is tucked into the croton collection near the gazebo — an area that functions as an informal croton showcase, with plants of all shapes, sizes, and leaf varieties on display. Easy to miss among the croton variety, but worth finding for the coral-red flowers and the striking star-shaped fruit capsules that follow.
wildlife_value: Hummingbirds are the primary flower visitors — the tubular red flowers are structurally suited to hovering birds. Butterflies also visit.
The ornamental fruit capsules split at maturity to release seeds. Birds occasionally eat the seeds.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Coral-red, tubular, in terminal clusters. Attractive to hummingbirds.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Star-shaped capsules, coral-red, splitting to release seeds. Ornamental.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Deeply dissected into narrow, finger-like lobes. Medium green. The finely cut leaf is the ID signature.

blocks.label: Sap
blocks.text: White milky latex when stems are cut or broken.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Upright shrub, 3 to 6 feet.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: The deeply dissected, finger-like leaf lobes are unlike most other shrubs in the collection. The coral-red flower clusters and the star-shaped fruit capsules. White sap from cut stems confirms euphorbia family membership.

size.habit: open, sparsely branched evergreen shrub or small tree with a smooth trunk

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: well-drained; tolerates poor and sandy soils
growing_conditions.drought_tolerance: high once established - stores water in the swollen trunk
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: frost-tender; damaged below about 40°F; USDA zones 10 to 11
growing_conditions.note: all parts toxic; the milky sap irritates skin and eyes - site away from heavy foot traffic and handle with care.

edibility.detail: Not edible. All parts toxic.

toxicity.people: Toxic. All parts contain toxic diterpenes typical of the spurge family. Milky sap causes skin and eye irritation. Seeds toxic if ingested. Do not eat.
toxicity.dogs: Toxic to dogs and cats. Ingestion causes vomiting, drooling, and diarrhea. Sap is an irritant. Keep pets away.

invasive.notes: Not on Florida's FISC invasive plant list. Not flagged by UF/IFAS.

species.category: Plants to Watch and Invasive Awareness
quick_hits: The fragrant white flower clusters in spring give the common name — the scent is genuinely sweet and carries some distance. The berries that follow are eaten by birds and are responsible for why Sweet Viburnum is now classified as a FISC Category I invasive in Florida.
Widely planted in Florida landscapes for decades before the invasive status was understood. It is still sold in some nurseries. The park specimen is maintained as an educational example.

The dense evergreen canopy, fast growth, and tolerance of shade, drought, and salt made it seem ideal for hedging and screening. Those same qualities now help it establish and spread in natural hammocks.
Florida native alternatives — Viburnum obovatum (Walter's Viburnum) — deliver similar landscape function without the invasive risk.
species.origin: Non-native. Native to Japan, China, Korea, and Southeast Asia. Member of Viburnaceae (formerly Adoxaceae). Introduced as a landscape plant and now invasive in Florida and several other southeastern states.
more_information: At Palma Sola, Sweet Viburnum lines the office and galleria, growing against a raised deck that sits six feet off the ground with a four-foot rail on top. The plants reach all of it and regularly push through the rails. Visitors who know this only as a tidy hedge shrub are consistently surprised by the scale. Once a year it gets a hard cut — far more severe than most people expect — and comes back fast and strong every time. Bright Futures high school volunteers do the cutback work to keep the walkway clear.
wildlife_value: The fruit is eaten by birds, which disperse seeds into natural areas — the mechanism of its invasive spread. The flowers attract bees and butterflies in spring. The dense evergreen growth provides nesting structure.
The wildlife value is real but the invasive consequence is serious. Birds spreading seeds into hammocks and natural areas do more long-term ecological harm than the nectar and fruit provide benefit.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, white, fragrant, in flat-topped clusters. Spring bloom.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Small, oval, red ripening to black. In clusters. Eaten by birds.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Opposite, glossy dark green, ovate with a finely toothed margin. Dense, persistent.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Evergreen shrub to small tree, 10 to 20 feet. Fast growing. Dense canopy.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: The fragrant white flower clusters in spring are the seasonal identification feature. The glossy dark opposite leaves are the year-round marker. Compare with Walter's Viburnum (Viburnum obovatum) — native alternative with smaller leaves and similar landscape function.

size.height_length: 10 to 20 feet; can reach about 30 feet
size.habit: large, dense evergreen shrub or small tree; commonly sheared as a tall hedge or screen
size.texture: medium to coarse - glossy leaves to 6 inches

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: wide range; moist, well-drained preferred; tolerates poor soils
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: USDA zones 8 to 10
growing_conditions.note: fast-growing and easily sheared, which is why it is so widely planted as a privacy hedge - the same vigor that lets it spread into natural areas.

edibility.detail: Fruit not typically consumed as food. Not considered toxic but not a food plant.

toxicity.people: Generally low toxicity to people. Fruit not considered seriously toxic but not a food plant. Avoid ingestion as a precaution.
toxicity.dogs: Mild caution. Fruit may cause mild stomach upset in dogs if eaten in quantity.

invasive.notes: Classified as a FISC Category I invasive and "Invasive/Not Recommended" by the UF/IFAS Assessment of Non-Native Plants. Spreads via bird-dispersed seeds into natural hammock communities. Still sold in some Florida nurseries despite invasive status. Kept here as an educational specimen. The native alternative for landscape use is Viburnum obovatum — Walter's Viburnum.

quick_hits: The color range of the Garden Croton is extraordinary by ornamental plant standards — a single leaf can include red, orange, yellow, green, and purple simultaneously, and no two plants look alike.

Native to Malaysia and the western Pacific islands, where the wild form is an understated green shrub. All the dramatic colors in cultivation come from centuries of selection and hybridization — there are thousands of named cultivars.
Despite the common name, this is not related to the weedy Croton genus of North American wildflowers. The name comes from a superficial resemblance.
Toxic — the sap and seeds contain diterpene esters. One of the more reliably toxic landscape plants in Florida.
species.origin: Non-native. Native to Malaysia, Indonesia, and the western Pacific islands. Member of Euphorbiaceae — the spurge family, same as Copperleaf, Chenille Plant, and Coral Plant in the collection.
more_information: Crotons are scattered throughout the park, most donated from volunteers' home gardens to anchor areas with year-round color. Near the gazebo, an informal croton showcase has developed — plants of all shapes, sizes, and leaf varieties on display together, a good spot to appreciate how much variety exists within a single species. A June 2026 addition fronts the left side of the cactus garden, brought and planted by a volunteer from Sarasota.
wildlife_value: Limited direct wildlife value. The flowers are inconspicuous. The toxic sap deters browsing by most animals.
Birds occasionally perch in the dense foliage. The primary ecological role in a Florida landscape is ornamental rather than wildlife-functional.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Inconspicuous, small, white, in spikes above the foliage. Not ornamentally significant.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: The display. Extremely variable — leathery, in shapes from oval to strap-like to lobed. Colors include combinations of green, yellow, orange, red, and purple, with patterns from solid to blotched to veined. No two cultivars look alike.

blocks.label: Sap
blocks.text: Milky white latex when stems or leaves are broken.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Upright evergreen shrub, 3 to 8 feet. Dense, colorful.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: The multicolored leaves are unmistakable — no other common Florida shrub produces this color range on a single leaf. The milky sap from broken stems confirms euphorbia family membership.

size.height_length: typically 3 to 8 feet in the landscape; can reach about 10 feet
size.habit: erect, bushy evergreen shrub

growing_conditions.light: bright light to full sun brings out the strongest leaf color; tolerates part shade with more muted color
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: moist, well-drained, fertile
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: frost-tender; damaged below about 40°F; USDA zones 10 to 11 (9B with protection)
growing_conditions.note: color is best in strong light; leaf drop follows cold snaps or sudden moves. Sap stains and irritates skin.

edibility.detail: Not edible. All parts toxic.

toxicity.people: Toxic. Contains diterpene esters in sap and seeds. Ingestion causes vomiting, diarrhea, and burning sensation in mouth and throat. Sap causes skin and eye irritation. Wash hands after handling.
toxicity.dogs: Toxic to dogs and cats. Ingestion causes drooling, vomiting, and diarrhea. Sap is a skin irritant. Keep pets away.

invasive.notes: Not on Florida's FISC invasive plant list. Not flagged by UF/IFAS.

quick_hits: Low in the middle of Bird of Paradise Island, beneath the towering birds, sits the most historically precious plant in the park: an Amazon Lily handed down through the Baden family since the 1880s.
It has survived nearly a century and a half the way heirloom plants do — cutting after cutting, bulb division after bulb division, passed from one generation to the next.
Hurricane saltwater recently flooded it down to a few bare stubs. It refused to quit, and is pushing back up again — a fragrant white-flowered Amazonian forest bulb that simply will not die.
Look for the placard that tells its story, tucked among the giant Bird of Paradise, a Yellow Elder, and a Geiger tree.
species.origin: Native to the shaded, humid forest floors of the western Amazon basin — Peru and Colombia, in the foothills of the Andes. A bulb of the amaryllis family adapted to deep dappled shade, which is why it thrives low and protected here on Bird of Paradise Island rather than out in the open sun.
more_information: The Amazon Lily is Palma Sola's living heirloom. The specimen low in the center of Bird of Paradise Island has been in the Baden family since the 1880s, kept alive across roughly a century and a half not as a single plant but as an unbroken chain of cuttings and bulb divisions — each generation lifting offsets from the last and replanting them. The plant you see is, in a real sense, the same plant a Baden tended before Florida was crisscrossed with highways. Nick Baden, one of the park's founders and now in his nineties, still comes to work; among other things he runs the tractor to gather the debris piles, secures the permits, and personally babysits the bonfires that keep the grounds clear. The lily is his family's thread running straight through the park's history.

Botanically it's a wonderful match for that role. Eucharis is a shade-loving bulb from the Amazonian forest floor, and like many bulbs it reproduces by offsets — small daughter bulbs that cluster around the parent — so it is almost made to be divided and shared. That same underground bulb is why it's so hard to kill: when hurricane saltwater flooded the island and burned the foliage down to a few bare stubs, the bulbs held on below ground and began pushing up new growth. Salt-scorched to nubs, it still won't quit.

When it's happy and in bloom, the reward is a cluster of pure white, fragrant, daffodil-like flowers held above broad, glossy, deep-green leaves — each bloom a ring of six tepals around a green-tinged central cup, sweetly scented like jasmine. It earns its other name, the Eucharist Lily, from that luminous white.

Its home on Bird of Paradise Island is a fine piece of park theater in itself: some twenty massive Bird of Paradise plants tower over it, with a Yellow Elder and a Geiger tree sharing the bed, and the small Amazon Lily and its storied placard set low in the middle — easy to walk past, impossible to forget once you know what it is.
wildlife_value: The Amazon Lily's fragrant white flowers can draw the occasional bee or butterfly, but it offers little nectar and isn't a significant wildlife plant in Florida — its tubular, scent-forward flowers are built for specific tropical pollinators back in its Amazonian home. Its value here is human and historical rather than ecological: a deep-shade groundcover bulb that anchors the understory of Bird of Paradise Island and carries 140 years of the park's family history. Most animals leave the bulb and foliage alone.

blocks.label: Reproduces readily by offsets
blocks.text: daughter bulbs that form around the parent — which is exactly how it has been propagated and passed down here for generations. Also grown from seed, but division is the traditional method. Slow, reliable, and tolerant of crowding (it actually blooms better slightly rootbound).

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Clusters of 3 to 6 pure white, fragrant flowers on a leafless stalk above the foliage; each flower has six spreading tepals around a green-tinged central staminal cup, daffodil-like. Sweetly scented. Blooms one or more times a year, often in the cooler months.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Broad, glossy, dark green, ribbed, somewhat like peace-lily or hosta foliage, rising from the bulb.

blocks.label: Habit
blocks.text: Clumping, evergreen, shade-loving bulb forming tight rosettes.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: On Bird of Paradise Island, look LOW and to the middle, beneath the giant Bird of Paradise and near the Yellow Elder and Geiger tree. Find the historical placard — that's your marker. Right now the lily may look like little more than a few stubs recovering from salt damage; that humble appearance is part of the story. In better seasons, watch for the broad glossy leaves and the fragrant white flower clusters held above them.

size.height: 1 to 2 feet of foliage; flower stalks rise 18-24 inches above the leaves
size.spread: clumping, slowly expanding by offset bulbs
size.habit: evergreen shade bulb in tight rosettes
size.texture: bold, glossy foliage; elegant white blooms

growing_conditions.light: dappled to deep shade (protect from harsh afternoon sun, which scorches leaves)
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: rich, moist, well-drained, organic
growing_conditions.drought_tolerance: moderate once established; prefers consistent moisture
growing_conditions.salt_tolerance: low (recently salt-flooded here and knocked back to stubs)
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: frost-tender; USDA zones 10-11 outdoors
growing_conditions.note: blooms better slightly crowded/rootbound; propagate and share by dividing offsets — the traditional method that has kept this specimen alive since the 1880s

edibility.detail: Not edible. Like other amaryllis-family bulbs, it contains alkaloids and should not be eaten.

toxicity.people: Mildly toxic. As a member of the amaryllis family, the bulb and foliage contain alkaloids (lycorine-type) that can cause nausea and vomiting if ingested. Not a serious hazard in a garden setting, but not for eating.
toxicity.dogs: Mildly toxic to dogs. Amaryllis-family bulbs can cause drooling, vomiting, and digestive upset if chewed or eaten — the bulb is the most concentrated part. Discourage digging and chewing.

invasive.notes: Not on Florida's FISC invasive lists. A slow, clumping shade bulb; no spread concern.

species.internal_notes:
PARK CONTEXT (Randy) — SHOWCASE/HERITAGE PLANT: Located low in the center of Bird of Paradise Island, beneath ~20 massive Bird of Paradise plants; bed also holds a Yellow Elder and a Geiger tree. In the Baden family since the 1880s, propagated cutting/division after division across generations. Recently salt-flooded by hurricane storm surge down to bare stubs but resprouting — 'it won't quit.' Nick Baden, a park founder, in his 90s, still works — runs the tractor to build debris piles, gets authority permits, and babysits the bonfire burns himself. There is a historical placard at the plant telling its story.

TO DO: Randy to photograph the placard. SPECIES TO VERIFY from placard — Amazon Lily may be Eucharis amazonica or the hybrid Eucharis x grandiflora (both share the common name). Sign Level set to Genus until confirmed.

INTERPRETIVE GOLD: Arguably the park's most story-rich specimen — heirloom continuity + founder + resilience. Strong candidate for featured signage and web storytelling.

CONSENT: Confirm Nick Baden is comfortable being named on public signage/web before publishing.

quick_hits: A Florida native shrub or small tree that earns its keep year-round. In late winter and early spring it covers itself in small white flower clusters, locally nicknamed Florida snow, that draw bees and butterflies.
The red-to-black drupes that follow are eaten by birds and other wildlife, and the dense, fine foliage gives songbirds, including cardinals, a favored nesting spot.

Adaptable from full sun to full shade, drought tolerant once established, and wind resistant - one of the most forgiving natives for a Florida landscape, often used as a hedge in place of non-native boxwood.
Walter's Viburnum is the native counterpart to the park's non-native Sweet Viburnum (PSBP-00119) - same family, but this one belongs here.
species.origin: Native to Florida and the southeastern coastal plain (South Carolina, Georgia, Alabama, and Florida, except the Keys). Found in pine flatwoods, hydric hammocks, riverbanks, swamp borders, and drier uplands. Member of Viburnaceae, the same family as the park's non-native Sweet Viburnum. (Older plant labels and field guides may place it in the honeysuckle family, Caprifoliaceae, or in Adoxaceae - it has since been moved to Viburnaceae.)
more_information: Walter's Viburnum is named for Thomas Walter (1740-1789), an English-born planter and botanist in South Carolina who described the species in his Flora Caroliniana. It is one of the most adaptable native shrubs in Florida - evergreen to semi-evergreen (it drops its old leaves late in winter, just as the new ones push out), growing from seasonally wet flatwoods to dry uplands, in full sun or full shade.

Its early-spring flush of small white flower clusters is showy enough to have earned the nickname Florida snow, and it arrives when little else in the flatwoods is blooming - making it a critical early-season pollen source for native bees emerging before the rest of the springtime buffet opens. Pollinators work the flowers for pollen more than nectar, since viburnum flowers are shallow and produce little nectar. The flat drupes that follow ripen from red to black through summer and fall and are taken by a range of birds.

Left alone, the plant forms a dense, rounded shrub that can sucker into a thicket and eventually grow into a small tree to about 20 feet, occasionally taller. It tolerates hard pruning, which is why it is widely used as a native hedge or screen - a Florida-friendly stand-in for clipped boxwood. Dwarf cultivars such as 'Densa' are also sold.
wildlife_value: A strong year-round wildlife plant. The spring flowers provide pollen and some nectar for bees and butterflies, and the species is a documented larval host for the spring azure butterfly (Celastrina ladon). The summer-to-fall drupes feed songbirds and other wildlife, and the dense, fine foliage is a favored nesting and cover site for birds, including cardinals.

Spring azure caterpillars run a striking partnership with ants: they secrete a sugary fluid that ants feed on, and in return the ants guard the caterpillars from wasps and spiders - a tiny hired bodyguard service playing out on the foliage.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, white, fragrant, in flat-topped clusters 2 to 3 inches across at the branch tips in late winter to early spring. Showy in mass.

blocks.label: A flower-cluster trick
blocks.text: Look closely at a flower cluster: the showy blooms around the outer edge are often larger but sterile, while the small fertile flowers sit in the center. The plant grows a billboard of bigger fake flowers to pull pollinators in from a distance, then puts them to work on the tiny real ones once they land.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: A flattened drupe ripening from green to red to black through summer and fall. Eaten by birds.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Small, dark green, leathery, ovate to spatulate (wider near the tip), about 1 to 2 inches long, opposite, with entire or slightly toothed margins; tiny glandular dots beneath. Evergreen to semi-evergreen, dropping old leaves late in winter as new growth appears.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Dense, fine-textured shrub to small tree, often suckering into a thicket; takes hard pruning as a hedge.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: How to spot it in the wild: a dense, small-leaved shrub smothered in small white flower clusters in early spring, followed by red-to-black berries. The fine foliage and thicket habit set it apart from the larger-leaved, non-native Sweet Viburnum.

size.height_length: commonly 8 to 20 feet; can reach about 25 to 30 feet as a small tree, or be kept low as a clipped hedge
size.spread: 6 to 10 feet, wider where it suckers
size.habit: dense, fine-textured evergreen to semi-evergreen shrub or small tree; suckers can form a colony

growing_conditions.light: full sun to full shade - one of the most light-adaptable natives
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: wide range; moist to seasonally wet sandy, clay, or calcareous soils, and drier uplands; tolerates poor soils
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: excellent; USDA zones 7 to 10
growing_conditions.note: tolerates hard pruning; suckers can be kept in check with timely pruning.

edibility.detail: Not a food plant for humans. The drupes are an important food for birds and other wildlife.

toxicity.people: No significant toxicity to people documented.
toxicity.dogs: No significant toxicity to dogs documented.

invasive.notes: Florida native. Not invasive.

species.internal_notes: PARK CONTEXT: Location to be confirmed. iNat observation exists. Story belongs here once location confirmed — likely the tall row around office/galleria previously attributed to Sweet Viburnum.

quick_hits: The leathery red fruit is packed with hundreds of juicy seeds, each wrapped in its own jewel-like sac of edible pulp.
It is one of the oldest cultivated fruits on Earth — grown and prized for more than 5,000 years.
The many-seeded fruit has long been a symbol of abundance and fertility across cultures from Persia to the Mediterranean.
The word "grenade" comes from the pomegranate — early grenades were named for the fruit they resembled, seeds and all.
species.origin: Native to a region stretching from Iran (Persia) through northern India and into Central Asia. Cultivated since antiquity, it spread across the Mediterranean, the Middle East, and eventually the warm Americas. It grows well in Florida's warm climate, though it fruits best where summers are drier.
more_information: The pomegranate is among the most ancient of cultivated fruits, with a history reaching back more than five thousand years across Persia, the Mediterranean, and South Asia. Its many seeds made it a near-universal symbol of abundance, fertility, and renewal, woven into mythology, art, and religious texts.

Each fruit is a tidy piece of engineering. Inside the tough rind, hundreds of seeds are each surrounded by a translucent sac of tart-sweet juice called an aril — the part you actually eat — separated by bitter white membranes.
There is a language footnote hiding in the fruit. The pomegranate's old French name, "pomme grenade" (seeded apple), gave us the word grenade for the seed-filled explosive it was thought to resemble.
wildlife_value: The bright tubular flowers are rich in nectar and draw bees, butterflies, and occasionally hummingbirds. Ripe and split fruit feeds birds and small mammals. The dense, twiggy shrub also offers nesting and shelter cover.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A deciduous large shrub or small tree, often multi-trunked, with thin branches and sometimes thorny twigs.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Showy, waxy, orange-red, trumpet-shaped blooms in late spring and summer.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Round, leathery-skinned, yellow-brown to deep red, crowned with the dried flower calyx; packed with seeds in juicy red arils.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Narrow, glossy, opposite, bronze-tinted when young, turning yellow before dropping.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A twiggy shrub with vivid orange-red flowers in summer and round, crown-tipped fruit later in the season. The little "crown" on top of the fruit is the giveaway.

size.habit: multi-trunked shrub or small tree, often suckering

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · slightly alkaline · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: fruits best in hot, drier summers; Florida humidity can reduce fruit quality

edibility.detail: Edible. The juicy arils around each seed are eaten fresh or juiced; the tart rind and membranes are not.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to people in normal use. The fruit is a common food.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. The fruit may cause mild stomach upset if a dog eats a large amount, especially the rind.

invasive.notes: Not invasive in Florida.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #372612057 (david_mcswain); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.

quick_hits: Ripe, the fruit looks like a green tomato but scoops out like rich chocolate pudding — earning it the name "chocolate pudding fruit."
There is no actual chocolate involved; the flavor and custard texture are entirely the fruit's own.
It must be eaten dead-ripe and soft. Unripe, it is hard, bitter, and astringent.
It is a persimmon relative, and that mahogany-dark, sweet flesh is the family's strangest member.
species.origin: Native to Mexico, Central America, and Colombia. Long cultivated in its homeland and now grown in tropical and subtropical regions, including South and Central Florida.
more_information: Black sapote is a persimmon — a member of the ebony family — with a fruit unlike any other. Green and tomato-like on the outside, it ripens internally to a soft, dark brown, custardy pulp that genuinely resembles chocolate pudding in look and texture, with a mild, sweet flavor.

Timing is everything. Picked or eaten too soon, the fruit is rock-hard, chalky, and mouth-puckeringly astringent. Allowed to soften until the skin browns and the flesh turns almost black and jelly-like, it becomes a dessert in its own right, often blended with a little citrus or folded into baked goods.
The tree is handsome and glossy-leaved, and its dark, dense wood links it to true ebony, the prized timber of the same genus.
wildlife_value: The small white flowers attract bees and other pollinators. Ripe and dropped fruit is taken by birds and small mammals, and the dense evergreen canopy offers cover.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A medium to large evergreen tree with a dense, rounded canopy of glossy leaves.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, white to greenish, tucked among the leaves.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Round, tomato-like, olive-green ripening to brown-black; soft dark pudding-like pulp inside.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Glossy, leathery, elliptic, dark green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Round green "tomatoes" hanging among glossy leaves. A ripe one is soft and dull-skinned; cut it to find the dark, custardy flesh.

size.habit: dense, rounded

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · acidic to slightly alkaline · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; prefers warm, frost-free sites

edibility.detail: Edible when fully ripe and soft — sweet, custard-like, eaten fresh or in desserts. Unripe fruit is hard, bitter, and astringent.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to people from the ripe fruit. Unripe fruit is unpleasantly astringent but not poisonous.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. Ripe flesh is harmless in small amounts; unripe fruit and seeds may upset a dog's stomach.

invasive.notes: Not invasive in Florida.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #372611941 (david_mcswain); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.

quick_hits: The avocado is a berry — botanically a single-seeded berry — despite its size and buttery, savory flesh.
Its flowers play a clever trick: each opens as female one day and male the next, timing the switch to avoid pollinating itself.
The creamy fruit is rich in healthy fats, which is unusual — most fruits store energy as sugar, not oil.
Delicious to people, but the leaves, skin, and pit contain persin, which is toxic to dogs, cats, and especially birds.
species.origin: Native to south-central Mexico and Central America, where it has been cultivated for thousands of years. Now grown worldwide in warm climates, with Florida (especially the south) a major U.S. growing region.
more_information: The avocado has been part of Mesoamerican life for millennia, long enough that its evolutionary partners — the giant ground sloths and other megafauna that once swallowed the fruit whole and dispersed its huge seed — have been extinct for thousands of years. The tree carries on, now spread by people instead.

Its flowers are a marvel of anti-inbreeding design. Each flower opens twice: functioning as female on the first opening and male on the second, with the timing staggered so a tree tends to shed pollen when its own flowers are receptive as female, encouraging cross-pollination between trees.

Unusually for a fruit, the avocado stores its energy as oil rather than sugar, which gives the flesh its rich, buttery quality. One caution worth posting: the leaves, bark, skin, and pit contain a compound called persin that is harmless to people in the fruit but toxic to many animals, birds most of all.
wildlife_value: The small flowers are visited by bees and other insects. Birds and mammals eat fallen fruit, and the large evergreen canopy provides shade, cover, and nesting sites.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A medium to large evergreen tree with a broad, often spreading or dense canopy.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, greenish-yellow, in large branched clusters; each flower opens as female then male.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: A large, pear-shaped to round berry with leathery green to black skin, buttery flesh, and one very large seed.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Large, glossy, elliptic, dark green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A leafy evergreen tree hung with pear-shaped fruit and a single oversized pit inside. Cut fruit shows the smooth pale-green to yellow flesh.

size.habit: spreading to upright, dense

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · slightly alkaline to acidic · must be well-drained
growing_conditions.note: hates wet feet — root rot is the main hazard in Florida; frost-sensitive

edibility.detail: Edible. The buttery flesh is widely eaten; the skin and pit are not eaten.

toxicity.people: No toxicity to people from the fruit. (The leaves and pit contain persin, but people eat only the flesh.)
toxicity.dogs: Use caution. Avocado leaves, skin, and pit contain persin, which can cause vomiting and diarrhea in dogs, and the large pit is a serious choking and obstruction hazard. Avocado is far more dangerous to birds (ASPCA).

invasive.notes: Not invasive in Florida.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #368070571 (denise_sosa); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.

quick_hits: This is the Florida state tree — the cabbage palm, found in every county in the state.
The growing bud at its center is the original "swamp cabbage," or heart of palm, a regional delicacy.
Harvesting that heart, though, kills the palm, since it is the single growing point the whole tree depends on.
Its criss-crossed leaf bases ("boots") create perches and pockets that ferns, vines, and small animals colonize.
species.origin: Native to the southeastern United States, throughout Florida and along the coastal plain. It grows in an exceptionally wide range of habitats — hammocks, marsh edges, dunes, and pine flatwoods — and tolerates salt, flooding, drought, and fire.
more_information: The cabbage palm is Florida's state tree and arguably its most characteristic, growing from the Keys to the Panhandle and shrugging off salt spray, flooding, drought, hurricanes, and fire. That toughness comes from a single, protected growing point (the "heart" or terminal bud) buried at the top of the trunk.

That heart is also the catch. Cut from the tree, the tender bud is the famous "swamp cabbage" or heart of palm — a Cracker Florida tradition still celebrated at festivals. But because a palm has only that one growing point, harvesting the heart kills the whole tree, which is why it is a treat to be taken thoughtfully and never from wild stands without care.

Older trunks may keep their crisscrossing old leaf bases, called boots, or shed them to a smooth gray column. Either way the palm is a habitat in itself — boots and leaf crowns host ferns, vines, insects, and nesting birds.
Two more notes worth knowing: it is one of the most hurricane-resistant trees in Florida, and it is the official state tree of two states — Florida and South Carolina, the "Palmetto State."
wildlife_value: The big sprays of white flowers are a major nectar source for bees (the basis of prized palm honey) and many insects. The black fruits feed raccoons, birds, and other wildlife, and the leaf-base "boots" and crown shelter ferns, insects, bats, and nesting birds.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A single-trunked fan palm with a rounded crown of large, arching leaves.

blocks.label: Trunk
blocks.text: Stout, gray, either smooth or covered in crisscrossing old leaf bases ("boots").

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Long, branching sprays of small, fragrant, creamy-white flowers in summer.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Small, round, shiny black drupes in large hanging clusters.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Large costapalmate (fan-like with an arching midrib), giving the leaf a folded, curved look.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A classic fan palm with a single gray trunk and curved, fan-shaped leaves. The arching midrib that runs partway into the fan separates it from true fan palms.

size.habit: single-trunked, rounded crown

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · clay · loam · acidic to alkaline · tolerates flooding and drought
growing_conditions.note: extremely tough — handles salt, flood, drought, and fire

edibility.detail: The growing bud (swamp cabbage / heart of palm) is edible and a Florida tradition — but harvesting it kills the palm. The small fruits are also edible but bland.

toxicity.people: No toxicity to people.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. Cabbage palm is not known to be harmful.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida — the state tree. Not invasive.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #370339474 (ruby_meador); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.

quick_hits: It looks like a stiff, low palm or fern, but it is a cycad — an ancient seed plant from the age of the dinosaurs.
The thick, leathery leaflets feel almost like cardboard, which is where the name comes from.
Every part is highly poisonous to people and pets — the seeds especially can be deadly to dogs.
Like all cycads, it partners with bacteria in special roots to pull nitrogen from the air, letting it thrive in poor soil.
species.origin: Native to a small area of eastern Mexico (Veracruz). Despite its limited wild range, it is one of the most widely planted ornamental cycads in warm climates, including Florida.
more_information: Cycads are living fossils — a lineage of seed plants that flourished alongside the dinosaurs and have changed little since. The cardboard cycad is one of the most popular in cultivation, prized for its low, symmetrical rosette of stiff, dark green, pinnate leaves whose thick, blunt leaflets have a distinctly papery, cardboard-like feel.

Beneath the handsome looks is a serious hazard. Every part of the plant contains cycasin and related toxins, and it is dangerously poisonous to people, dogs, cats, and livestock. The seeds are the most toxic part and are a frequent cause of severe, often fatal poisoning in dogs that chew them — a genuine concern wherever pets and cardboard cycads share a space.

Like other cycads, it forms specialized upward-growing "coralloid" roots that house nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria, a partnership that lets it thrive in poor, sandy soils. It also bears its seeds in cones rather than flowers, on separate male and female plants.

It also has a Florida cousin worth knowing. The cardboard cycad's genus, Zamia, includes the only cycad native to the United States — the coontie (Zamia integrifolia), a Florida native and the sole host plant of the atala butterfly. When coontie was nearly wiped out by over-harvesting for its starch ("Florida arrowroot"), the atala was thought extinct — until coontie was replanted across South Florida and the butterfly came roaring back, one of the state's great conservation comebacks.
wildlife_value: Wildlife value is limited by the plant's toxicity. In its native range, specialized cycad-feeding insects and beetles pollinate the cones; the toxic seeds are not a safe food source for local wildlife and pets.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A low, stiff, palm-like cycad with a short, often partly buried trunk and a symmetrical rosette of leaves.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Pinnate, dark green, with thick, leathery, blunt, cardboard-textured leaflets; new leaves emerge in a flush.

blocks.label: Reproduction
blocks.text: By cones, not flowers; male and female cones on separate plants.

blocks.label: Seeds
blocks.text: Borne in the female cone; large and highly toxic.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A short, stiff rosette of fern- or palm-like leaves with notably thick, firm, blunt leaflets. The cardboard feel of the leaflets and the cone (not flower) is the tell.

size.habit: low, spreading rosette

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · well-drained; tolerates poor soil
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive in hard freezes; very low-maintenance once established

edibility.detail: Not edible — highly toxic. No part of the plant should ever be eaten.

toxicity.people: Highly toxic if ingested. Contains cycasin and related toxins that can cause severe vomiting, liver damage, and neurological effects. The seeds are the most dangerous part. Seek immediate medical care if any part is eaten.
toxicity.dogs: Highly toxic to dogs — cycads (Zamia and relatives) are listed as toxic by ASPCA and are a leading cause of fatal plant poisoning in dogs. The seeds are especially deadly, causing liver failure. Keep pets well away and pick up any dropped seeds.

invasive.notes: Not invasive in Florida.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #372611510 (harrisonmello); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.

quick_hits: It looks like a white-flowered cousin of the orange Geiger tree — and it is, sharing the same rough, sandpapery leaves.
The showy white trumpet flowers have a soft yellow throat and bloom much of the year.
The small fruits that follow look like olives, giving it the name "Texas olive," though it is not a true olive.
It is notably cold-hardy for a tropical-looking tree, tolerating more frost than its orange Geiger relative.
species.origin: Native to southern Texas and northeastern Mexico. Grown as an ornamental flowering tree across the warm South, including Florida, and valued for its toughness and long bloom.
more_information: Texas olive — anacahuita in Mexico, where it is also native — is the white-flowered counterpart to the orange Geiger tree (Cordia sebestena), and the family resemblance is immediate: the same broad, stiff, sandpapery leaves, the same trumpet-shaped flowers in showy clusters — only here the blooms are crisp white with a yellow throat. It flowers heavily through much of the warm season.

The "olive" in Texas olive comes from the fruit, a small whitish drupe that resembles an olive but is unrelated to true olives. The fruits are edible in small amounts and have been used to make jellies and preserves, though eating many can be mildly intoxicating or laxative, and livestock that browse heavily on the foliage can be affected.
Its real advantage in the landscape is toughness: it handles heat, drought, and poor soil, and tolerates more cold than the tender orange Geiger, making it a hardier choice for a tropical look.
wildlife_value: The nectar-rich white flowers draw bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds over a long bloom season. The fruits are eaten by birds and other wildlife, and the dense canopy offers cover.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A small evergreen tree or large shrub with a rounded, dense crown.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Showy clusters of white trumpet-shaped blooms with yellow throats, much of the year.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Small, whitish, olive-like drupes.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Large, broad, stiff, dark green, with a rough sandpapery texture.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: White trumpet flowers with yellow throats on a shrubby tree with rough, sandpapery leaves — like a white-flowered Geiger tree. Run a thumb across a leaf to feel the grit.

size.habit: rounded, dense

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · limestone · alkaline tolerant · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: more cold-hardy than the orange Geiger tree; tolerates heat and poor soil

edibility.detail: The olive-like fruit is edible in small amounts (used for jellies), but large quantities can be mildly intoxicating or laxative. Not a casual trail nibble.

toxicity.people: Low toxicity to people. Small amounts of fruit are harmless; large amounts may cause mild intoxication or a laxative effect.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. Not known to be seriously harmful, though large amounts of fruit could upset a dog's stomach.

invasive.notes: Not considered invasive in Florida.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #370299582 (randall_carter); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.

quick_hits: A tough, silvery-leaved Florida native that thrives right at the salty edge where most plants cannot.
Its cheerful yellow daisy flowers bloom much of the year along marsh and shoreline margins.
The thick, succulent gray-green leaves store water and shed salt — built-in coastal survival gear.
It spreads into dense, soil-holding colonies that help anchor shorelines against erosion.
species.origin: Native to the coastal southeastern United States, including Florida, and the Gulf and Caribbean coasts. A characteristic plant of salt marshes, mangrove edges, and brackish shorelines.
more_information: Sea ox-eye daisy is a salt-marsh specialist — a low, woody Florida native that grows where the ground is salty, wet, and punishing for ordinary plants. Its thick, fleshy, silvery-gray leaves are adapted to store water and cope with salt, giving the plant a frosted, coastal look.
Through much of the year it carries bright yellow, daisy-like flowers that stand out against the gray foliage and feed coastal pollinators. After flowering, the seed heads become small and burr-like.

Its real ecological job is holding ground. Sea ox-eye spreads by rhizomes into dense colonies that bind soil at the marsh and shoreline edge, buffering wave energy and resisting erosion — quiet, essential work in the transition zone between land and water.

There is no better plant to meet at this park: Palma Sola Botanical Park sits right beside Palma Sola Bay, and sea ox-eye is a plant of exactly that salt-marsh and shoreline edge — the same species holding the soil along the bay just outside the gates. It is a go-to plant for Tampa Bay–area shoreline restoration for that very reason.
wildlife_value: The yellow flowers provide nectar for bees, butterflies, and other coastal pollinators much of the year, and the seeds feed birds. Its dense colonies stabilize shorelines and offer cover for small marsh animals and insects.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A low, woody, spreading native subshrub forming dense colonies.

blocks.label: Flowers
